revision.text:
{{short description|Former Shinto shrine in Taipei, Taiwan}} [[File:Painting of Taiwan Grand Shrine.jpg|thumb|300px|A painting of Taiwan Grand Shrine during [[Taiwan under Japanese rule|Japanese colonial rule]]. At the bottom of the painting is the Keelung River and in the lower-right corner the Meiji Bridge is partially visible.]]
The '''Taiwan Grand Shrine''' ({{CJKV|c=臺灣神宮|r=Taiwan Jingū|p=Táiwān Shéngōng}}) was the highest ranking Japanese [[Shinto shrine]] in Taiwan during [[Taiwan under Japanese rule|Japanese colonial rule]]. It was located in [[Taipei|Taihoku]], [[Taiwan]] (now [[Zhongshan District, Taipei]]). Among the officially sanctioned Shinto shrines in Taiwan, Taiwan Grand Shrine held the highest rank of them all. The [[Grand Hotel (Taipei City)|Grand Hotel]] stands at the shrine's former site.
== History == [[File:Taiwan-Grand-Shrine-Jingu-Map-1920s.png|thumb|300px|Taiwan Grand Shrine Map (1920s) records the location of each object and detailed information.]] Following the death of [[Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|Prince Yoshihisa]] in 1895, the [[Governor-General of Taiwan]] [[Nogi Maresuke]] began planning for a shrine in Yoshihisa's honor. Originally, the plan was to construct the shrine at Yuanshan Park ({{lang|zh-Hant|圓山公園}}, now part of [[Taipei Expo Park]]); however, Nogi's successor [[Kodama Gentarō]] and chief planner [[Gotō Shinpei]] decided to move it across the [[Keelung River]] to Jiantan Mountain ({{lang|zh-Hant|劍潭山}}) for the site's higher elevation. The vantage point would allow the shrine to overlook the entire city, making it symbolic for the Japanese Empire's colonial power.<ref name="lee">{{cite book |author=李東明 |title=永遠的北淡線 |publisher=玉山社 |isbn=957-8246-32-3 |pages=88–90 |language=zh-tw |chapter=宮之下 - 台灣神社下的火車站|year=2000 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |title=第四章 日治時期臺灣各級神社的選址與設域的空間特性 |pages=137–140 |url=http://rportal.lib.ntnu.edu.tw/bitstream/20.500.12235/94400/4/n088723001004.pdf |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |publisher=[[National Taiwan Normal University]] |language=zh-tw}}</ref> The construction lasted between 1900 and 1901, and the completed shrine was dedicated to Yoshihisa and the {{nihongo|Kaitaku Sanjin|開拓三神|Three Kami Deities of Pioneering}}.<ref name="hsu">{{cite web |author1=許書維 |title=臺灣神社誌 |url=https://www.th.gov.tw/epaper/site/page/78/1078 |website=國史館臺灣文獻館 |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw}}</ref> In 1915, a railway station named {{nihongo|Miyanoshita Station|宮ノ下乘降場||now [[Jiantan metro station]]}} was placed at the foot of Jiantan Mountain to serve the shrine.<ref name="lee"/> On April 12, 1923, [[Crown Prince]] [[Hirohito]], who would become Emperor Shōwa three years later, embarked on a two-week tour of Taiwan. In preparation for his visit to the shrine, {{nihongo|Chokushi Road|勅使街道|Chokushi kaidō|present-day Chungshan North Road}} was created leading up to the shrine from the city, with the {{nihongo|Meiji Bridge|明治橋}} crossing the Keelung River.<ref>{{cite web |author1=一流人 |title=我們住在「中國的地圖」裡？台北路名藏著滿滿的台灣過往 |url=https://www.gvm.com.tw/article/71003 |website=遠見 |publisher=天下文化 |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw |date=February 21, 2020}}</ref>
The shrine was elevated in rank to Grand Shrine (''jingū'') in 1944 ([[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] 19) when [[Amaterasu]] was enshrined, making it the highest-ranking shrine in Taiwan.<ref name="hsu"/> The opening ceremony of the new shrine was planned to be on October 28. However, on October 23, 1944, a cargo plane lost control and crashed atop the mountain where the Taiwan Grand Shrine was located, heavily damaging roughly half of the shrine.<ref>{{cite web |author1=郭俊麟 |title=淺談臺灣今昔地圖與歷史航照在中學地理資訊教育的意義 |url=http://epaper.ntu.edu.tw/view.php?id=10773 |website=GIS 教育推廣 電子報 |publisher=[[National Taiwan University]] |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw}}</ref> The shrine was never fully repaired due to [[Surrender of Japan|Japan's surrender after World War II]], and much of the shrine's materials were taken for construction projects elsewhere. In its place, the [[Grand Hotel (Taipei City)|Grand Hotel]] was constructed in 1952, where it remains as a prominent landmark of the city.<ref name="hsu"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Taiwan Grand Shrine.jpg|A [[postcard]] issued by the [[Governor-General of Taiwan]] of Taiwan Grand Shrine. The [[Imperial Seal of Japan|Imperial Seal]] is visible in the upper-right section. File:Taiwan_(Japanese_Colony)_1932_bank_note_-_10_yen_(front).jpg|Taiwan banknotes (10 yen banknotes) during the Japanese colonial era with the Taiwan Grand Shrine printed File:1923年日本皇太子裕仁訪拜臺灣神社_Japanese_Crown_Prince_Hirohito_on_the_way_to_Taiwan_Grand_Shrine.jpg|Prince Hirohito visits the Taiwan Grand Shrine during the Crown Prince Hirohito's visit to Taiwan (April 17, 1923) File:Copper cattle of Taiwan Jinja.JPG|This copper bull was originally located at Taiwan Grand Shrine but was moved to in front of [[National Taiwan Museum]] after the end of World War II. </gallery>
==See also== *[[Shintoism in Taiwan]] *[[List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan]]
==References== {{reflist}}
{{coord missing|Taiwan}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Taiwan]] [[Category:1901 establishments in Taiwan]] [[Category:1945 disestablishments in Taiwan]] [[Category:Taiwan under Japanese rule]] [[Category:20th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Shinmei shrines]] {{Shinmei shrines}}

File:Taiwan_(Japanese_Colony)_1932_bank_note_-_10_yen_(front).jpg|Taiwan banknotes (10 yen banknotes) during the Japanese colonial era with the Taiwan Grand Shrine printed File:1923年日本皇太子裕仁訪拜臺灣神社_Japanese_Crown_Prince_Hirohito_on_the_way_to_Taiwan_Grand_Shrine.jpg|Prince Hirohito visits the Taiwan Grand Shrine during the Crown Prince Hirohito's visit to Taiwan (April 17, 1923) File:Copper cattle of Taiwan Jinja.JPG|This copper bull was originally located at Taiwan Grand Shrine but was moved to in front of [[National Taiwan Museum]] after the end of World War II. </gallery>

The [[Grand Hotel (Taipei City)|Grand Hotel]] stands at the shrine's former site.
== History == [[File:Taiwan-Grand-Shrine-Jingu-Map-1920s.png|thumb|300px|Taiwan Grand Shrine Map (1920s) records the location of each object and detailed information.]] Following the death of [[Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|Prince Yoshihisa]] in 1895, the [[Governor-General of Taiwan]] [[Nogi Maresuke]] began planning for a shrine in Yoshihisa's honor. Originally, the plan was to construct the shrine at Yuanshan Park ({{lang|zh-Hant|圓山公園}}, now part of [[Taipei Expo Park]]); however, Nogi's successor [[Kodama Gentarō]] and chief planner [[Gotō Shinpei]] decided to move it across the [[Keelung River]] to Jiantan Mountain ({{lang|zh-Hant|劍潭山}}) for the site's higher elevation. The vantage point would allow the shrine to overlook the entire city, making it symbolic for the Japanese Empire's colonial power.<ref name="lee">{{cite book |author=李東明 |title=永遠的北淡線 |publisher=玉山社 |isbn=957-8246-32-3 |pages=88–90 |language=zh-tw |chapter=宮之下 - 台灣神社下的火車站|year=2000 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |title=第四章 日治時期臺灣各級神社的選址與設域的空間特性 |pages=137–140 |url=http://rportal.lib.ntnu.edu.tw/bitstream/20.500.12235/94400/4/n088723001004.pdf |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |publisher=[[National Taiwan Normal University]] |language=zh-tw}}</ref> The construction lasted between 1900 and 1901, and the completed shrine was dedicated to Yoshihisa and the {{nihongo|Kaitaku Sanjin|開拓三神|Three Kami Deities of Pioneering}}.<ref name="hsu">{{cite web |author1=許書維 |title=臺灣神社誌 |url=https://www.th.gov.tw/epaper/site/page/78/1078 |website=國史館臺灣文獻館 |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw}}</ref> In 1915, a railway station named {{nihongo|Miyanoshita Station|宮ノ下乘降場||now [[Jiantan metro station]]}} was placed at the foot of Jiantan Mountain to serve the shrine.<ref name="lee"/> On April 12, 1923, [[Crown Prince]] [[Hirohito]], who would become Emperor Shōwa three years later, embarked on a two-week tour of Taiwan. In preparation for his visit to the shrine, {{nihongo|Chokushi Road|勅使街道|Chokushi kaidō|present-day Chungshan North Road}} was created leading up to the shrine from the city, with the {{nihongo|Meiji Bridge|明治橋}} crossing the Keelung River.<ref>{{cite web |author1=一流人 |title=我們住在「中國的地圖」裡？台北路名藏著滿滿的台灣過往 |url=https://www.gvm.com.tw/article/71003 |website=遠見 |publisher=天下文化 |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw |date=February 21, 2020}}</ref>
The shrine was elevated in rank to Grand Shrine (''jingū'') in 1944 ([[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] 19) when [[Amaterasu]] was enshrined, making it the highest-ranking shrine in Taiwan.<ref name="hsu"/> The opening ceremony of the new shrine was planned to be on October 28. However, on October 23, 1944, a cargo plane lost control and crashed atop the mountain where the Taiwan Grand Shrine was located, heavily damaging roughly half of the shrine.<ref>{{cite web |author1=郭俊麟 |title=淺談臺灣今昔地圖與歷史航照在中學地理資訊教育的意義 |url=http://epaper.ntu.edu.tw/view.php?id=10773 |website=GIS 教育推廣 電子報 |publisher=[[National Taiwan University]] |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw}}</ref> The shrine was never fully repaired due to [[Surrender of Japan|Japan's surrender after World War II]], and much of the shrine's materials were taken for construction projects elsewhere. In its place, the [[Grand Hotel (Taipei City)|Grand Hotel]] was constructed in 1952, where it remains as a prominent landmark of the city.<ref name="hsu"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Taiwan Grand Shrine.jpg|A [[postcard]] issued by the [[Governor-General of Taiwan]] of Taiwan Grand Shrine. The [[Imperial Seal of Japan|Imperial Seal]] is visible in the upper-right section. File:Taiwan_(Japanese_Colony)_1932_bank_note_-_10_yen_(front).jpg|Taiwan banknotes (10 yen banknotes) during the Japanese colonial era with the Taiwan Grand Shrine printed File:1923年日本皇太子裕仁訪拜臺灣神社_Japanese_Crown_Prince_Hirohito_on_the_way_to_Taiwan_Grand_Shrine.jpg|Prince Hirohito visits the Taiwan Grand Shrine during the Crown Prince Hirohito's visit to Taiwan (April 17, 1923) File:Copper cattle of Taiwan Jinja.JPG|This copper bull was originally located at Taiwan Grand Shrine but was moved to in front of [[National Taiwan Museum]] after the end of World War II. </gallery>
==See also== *[[Shintoism in Taiwan]] *[[List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan]]

In preparation for his visit to the shrine, {{nihongo|Chokushi Road|勅使街道|Chokushi kaidō|present-day Chungshan North Road}} was created leading up to the shrine from the city, with the {{nihongo|Meiji Bridge|明治橋}} crossing the Keelung River.<ref>{{cite web |author1=一流人 |title=我們住在「中國的地圖」裡？台北路名藏著滿滿的台灣過往 |url=https://www.gvm.com.tw/article/71003 |website=遠見 |publisher=天下文化 |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw |date=February 21, 2020}}</ref>
The shrine was elevated in rank to Grand Shrine (''jingū'') in 1944 ([[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] 19) when [[Amaterasu]] was enshrined, making it the highest-ranking shrine in Taiwan.<ref name="hsu"/> The opening ceremony of the new shrine was planned to be on October 28. However, on October 23, 1944, a cargo plane lost control and crashed atop the mountain where the Taiwan Grand Shrine was located, heavily damaging roughly half of the shrine.<ref>{{cite web |author1=郭俊麟 |title=淺談臺灣今昔地圖與歷史航照在中學地理資訊教育的意義 |url=http://epaper.ntu.edu.tw/view.php?id=10773 |website=GIS 教育推廣 電子報 |publisher=[[National Taiwan University]] |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw}}</ref> The shrine was never fully repaired due to [[Surrender of Japan|Japan's surrender after World War II]], and much of the shrine's materials were taken for construction projects elsewhere. The [[Imperial Seal of Japan|Imperial Seal]] is visible in the upper-right section. == History == [[File:Taiwan-Grand-Shrine-Jingu-Map-1920s.png|thumb|300px|Taiwan Grand Shrine Map (1920s) records the location of each object and detailed information.]] Following the death of [[Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|Prince Yoshihisa]] in 1895, the [[Governor-General of Taiwan]] [[Nogi Maresuke]] began planning for a shrine in Yoshihisa's honor. Originally, the plan was to construct the shrine at Yuanshan Park ({{lang|zh-Hant|圓山公園}}, now part of [[Taipei Expo Park]]); however, Nogi's successor [[Kodama Gentarō]] and chief planner [[Gotō Shinpei]] decided to move it across the [[Keelung River]] to Jiantan Mountain ({{lang|zh-Hant|劍潭山}}) for the site's higher elevation. The vantage point would allow the shrine to overlook the entire city, making it symbolic for the Japanese Empire's colonial power.<ref name="lee">{{cite book |author=李東明 |title=永遠的北淡線 |publisher=玉山社 |isbn=957-8246-32-3 |pages=88–90 |language=zh-tw |chapter=宮之下 - 台灣神社下的火車站|year=2000 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |title=第四章 日治時期臺灣各級神社的選址與設域的空間特性 |pages=137–140 |url=http://rportal.lib.ntnu.edu.tw/bitstream/20.500.12235/94400/4/n088723001004.pdf |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |publisher=[[National Taiwan Normal University]] |language=zh-tw}}</ref> The construction lasted between 1900 and 1901, and the completed shrine was dedicated to Yoshihisa and the {{nihongo|Kaitaku Sanjin|開拓三神|Three Kami Deities of Pioneering}}.<ref name="hsu">{{cite web |author1=許書維 |title=臺灣神社誌 |url=https://www.th.gov.tw/epaper/site/page/78/1078 |website=國史館臺灣文獻館 |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw}}</ref> In 1915, a railway station named {{nihongo|Miyanoshita Station|宮ノ下乘降場||now [[Jiantan metro station]]}} was placed at the foot of Jiantan Mountain to serve the shrine.<ref name="lee"/> On April 12, 1923, [[Crown Prince]] [[Hirohito]], who would become Emperor Shōwa three years later, embarked on a two-week tour of Taiwan. In preparation for his visit to the shrine, {{nihongo|Chokushi Road|勅使街道|Chokushi kaidō|present-day Chungshan North Road}} was created leading up to the shrine from the city, with the {{nihongo|Meiji Bridge|明治橋}} crossing the Keelung River.<ref>{{cite web |author1=一流人 |title=我們住在「中國的地圖」裡？台北路名藏著滿滿的台灣過往 |url=https://www.gvm.com.tw/article/71003 |website=遠見 |publisher=天下文化 |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw |date=February 21, 2020}}</ref>
The shrine was elevated in rank to Grand Shrine (''jingū'') in 1944 ([[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] 19) when [[Amaterasu]] was enshrined, making it the highest-ranking shrine in Taiwan.<ref name="hsu"/> The opening ceremony of the new shrine was planned to be on October 28. However, on October 23, 1944, a cargo plane lost control and crashed atop the mountain where the Taiwan Grand Shrine was located, heavily damaging roughly half of the shrine.<ref>{{cite web |author1=郭俊麟 |title=淺談臺灣今昔地圖與歷史航照在中學地理資訊教育的意義 |url=http://epaper.ntu.edu.tw/view.php?id=10773 |website=GIS 教育推廣 電子報 |publisher=[[National Taiwan University]] |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw}}</ref> The shrine was never fully repaired due to [[Surrender of Japan|Japan's surrender after World War II]], and much of the shrine's materials were taken for construction projects elsewhere. In its place, the [[Grand Hotel (Taipei City)|Grand Hotel]] was constructed in 1952, where it remains as a prominent landmark of the city.<ref name="hsu"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Taiwan Grand Shrine.jpg|A [[postcard]] issued by the [[Governor-General of Taiwan]] of Taiwan Grand Shrine. The [[Imperial Seal of Japan|Imperial Seal]] is visible in the upper-right section. File:Taiwan_(Japanese_Colony)_1932_bank_note_-_10_yen_(front).jpg|Taiwan banknotes (10 yen banknotes) during the Japanese colonial era with the Taiwan Grand Shrine printed File:1923年日本皇太子裕仁訪拜臺灣神社_Japanese_Crown_Prince_Hirohito_on_the_way_to_Taiwan_Grand_Shrine.jpg|Prince Hirohito visits the Taiwan Grand Shrine during the Crown Prince Hirohito's visit to Taiwan (April 17, 1923) File:Copper cattle of Taiwan Jinja.JPG|This copper bull was originally located at Taiwan Grand Shrine but was moved to in front of [[National Taiwan Museum]] after the end of World War II. </gallery>
==See also== *[[Shintoism in Taiwan]] *[[List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan]]

The [[Grand Hotel (Taipei City)|Grand Hotel]] stands at the shrine's former site.

In preparation for his visit to the shrine, {{nihongo|Chokushi Road|勅使街道|Chokushi kaidō|present-day Chungshan North Road}} was created leading up to the shrine from the city, with the {{nihongo|Meiji Bridge|明治橋}} crossing the Keelung River.<ref>{{cite web |author1=一流人 |title=我們住在「中國的地圖」裡？台北路名藏著滿滿的台灣過往 |url=https://www.gvm.com.tw/article/71003 |website=遠見 |publisher=天下文化 |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw |date=February 21, 2020}}</ref>

However, on October 23, 1944, a cargo plane lost control and crashed atop the mountain where the Taiwan Grand Shrine was located, heavily damaging roughly half of the shrine.<ref>{{cite web |author1=郭俊麟 |title=淺談臺灣今昔地圖與歷史航照在中學地理資訊教育的意義 |url=http://epaper.ntu.edu.tw/view.php?id=10773 |website=GIS 教育推廣 電子報 |publisher=[[National Taiwan University]] |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw}}</ref> The shrine was never fully repaired due to [[Surrender of Japan|Japan's surrender after World War II]], and much of the shrine's materials were taken for construction projects elsewhere. The [[Imperial Seal of Japan|Imperial Seal]] is visible in the upper-right section. == History == [[File:Taiwan-Grand-Shrine-Jingu-Map-1920s.png|thumb|300px|Taiwan Grand Shrine Map (1920s) records the location of each object and detailed information.]] Following the death of [[Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|Prince Yoshihisa]] in 1895, the [[Governor-General of Taiwan]] [[Nogi Maresuke]] began planning for a shrine in Yoshihisa's honor. Originally, the plan was to construct the shrine at Yuanshan Park ({{lang|zh-Hant|圓山公園}}, now part of [[Taipei Expo Park]]); however, Nogi's successor [[Kodama Gentarō]] and chief planner [[Gotō Shinpei]] decided to move it across the [[Keelung River]] to Jiantan Mountain ({{lang|zh-Hant|劍潭山}}) for the site's higher elevation. The vantage point would allow the shrine to overlook the entire city, making it symbolic for the Japanese Empire's colonial power.<ref name="lee">{{cite book |author=李東明 |title=永遠的北淡線 |publisher=玉山社 |isbn=957-8246-32-3 |pages=88–90 |language=zh-tw |chapter=宮之下 - 台灣神社下的火車站|year=2000 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |title=第四章 日治時期臺灣各級神社的選址與設域的空間特性 |pages=137–140 |url=http://rportal.lib.ntnu.edu.tw/bitstream/20.500.12235/94400/4/n088723001004.pdf |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |publisher=[[National Taiwan Normal University]] |language=zh-tw}}</ref> The construction lasted between 1900 and 1901, and the completed shrine was dedicated to Yoshihisa and the {{nihongo|Kaitaku Sanjin|開拓三神|Three Kami Deities of Pioneering}}.<ref name="hsu">{{cite web |author1=許書維 |title=臺灣神社誌 |url=https://www.th.gov.tw/epaper/site/page/78/1078 |website=國史館臺灣文獻館 |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw}}</ref> In 1915, a railway station named {{nihongo|Miyanoshita Station|宮ノ下乘降場||now [[Jiantan metro station]]}} was placed at the foot of Jiantan Mountain to serve the shrine.<ref name="lee"/> On April 12, 1923, [[Crown Prince]] [[Hirohito]], who would become Emperor Shōwa three years later, embarked on a two-week tour of Taiwan. In preparation for his visit to the shrine, {{nihongo|Chokushi Road|勅使街道|Chokushi kaidō|present-day Chungshan North Road}} was created leading up to the shrine from the city, with the {{nihongo|Meiji Bridge|明治橋}} crossing the Keelung River.<ref>{{cite web |author1=一流人 |title=我們住在「中國的地圖」裡？台北路名藏著滿滿的台灣過往 |url=https://www.gvm.com.tw/article/71003 |website=遠見 |publisher=天下文化 |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw |date=February 21, 2020}}</ref>
The shrine was elevated in rank to Grand Shrine (''jingū'') in 1944 ([[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] 19) when [[Amaterasu]] was enshrined, making it the highest-ranking shrine in Taiwan.<ref name="hsu"/> The opening ceremony of the new shrine was planned to be on October 28. However, on October 23, 1944, a cargo plane lost control and crashed atop the mountain where the Taiwan Grand Shrine was located, heavily damaging roughly half of the shrine.<ref>{{cite web |author1=郭俊麟 |title=淺談臺灣今昔地圖與歷史航照在中學地理資訊教育的意義 |url=http://epaper.ntu.edu.tw/view.php?id=10773 |website=GIS 教育推廣 電子報 |publisher=[[National Taiwan University]] |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw}}</ref> The shrine was never fully repaired due to [[Surrender of Japan|Japan's surrender after World War II]], and much of the shrine's materials were taken for construction projects elsewhere. In its place, the [[Grand Hotel (Taipei City)|Grand Hotel]] was constructed in 1952, where it remains as a prominent landmark of the city.<ref name="hsu"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Taiwan Grand Shrine.jpg|A [[postcard]] issued by the [[Governor-General of Taiwan]] of Taiwan Grand Shrine. The [[Imperial Seal of Japan|Imperial Seal]] is visible in the upper-right section. File:Taiwan_(Japanese_Colony)_1932_bank_note_-_10_yen_(front).jpg|Taiwan banknotes (10 yen banknotes) during the Japanese colonial era with the Taiwan Grand Shrine printed File:1923年日本皇太子裕仁訪拜臺灣神社_Japanese_Crown_Prince_Hirohito_on_the_way_to_Taiwan_Grand_Shrine.jpg|Prince Hirohito visits the Taiwan Grand Shrine during the Crown Prince Hirohito's visit to Taiwan (April 17, 1923) File:Copper cattle of Taiwan Jinja.JPG|This copper bull was originally located at Taiwan Grand Shrine but was moved to in front of [[National Taiwan Museum]] after the end of World War II. </gallery>
==See also== *[[Shintoism in Taiwan]] *[[List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan]]

At the bottom of the painting is the Keelung River and in the lower-right corner the Meiji Bridge is partially visible.]]

In preparation for his visit to the shrine, {{nihongo|Chokushi Road|勅使街道|Chokushi kaidō|present-day Chungshan North Road}} was created leading up to the shrine from the city, with the {{nihongo|Meiji Bridge|明治橋}} crossing the Keelung River.<ref>{{cite web |author1=一流人 |title=我們住在「中國的地圖」裡？台北路名藏著滿滿的台灣過往 |url=https://www.gvm.com.tw/article/71003 |website=遠見 |publisher=天下文化 |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw |date=February 21, 2020}}</ref>

However, on October 23, 1944, a cargo plane lost control and crashed atop the mountain where the Taiwan Grand Shrine was located, heavily damaging roughly half of the shrine.<ref>{{cite web |author1=郭俊麟 |title=淺談臺灣今昔地圖與歷史航照在中學地理資訊教育的意義 |url=http://epaper.ntu.edu.tw/view.php?id=10773 |website=GIS 教育推廣 電子報 |publisher=[[National Taiwan University]] |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw}}</ref> The shrine was never fully repaired due to [[Surrender of Japan|Japan's surrender after World War II]], and much of the shrine's materials were taken for construction projects elsewhere. ==Gallery== <gallery> File:Taiwan Grand Shrine.jpg|A [[postcard]] issued by the [[Governor-General of Taiwan]] of Taiwan Grand Shrine. The [[Imperial Seal of Japan|Imperial Seal]] is visible in the upper-right section. File:Taiwan_(Japanese_Colony)_1932_bank_note_-_10_yen_(front).jpg|Taiwan banknotes (10 yen banknotes) during the Japanese colonial era with the Taiwan Grand Shrine printed File:1923年日本皇太子裕仁訪拜臺灣神社_Japanese_Crown_Prince_Hirohito_on_the_way_to_Taiwan_Grand_Shrine.jpg|Prince Hirohito visits the Taiwan Grand Shrine during the Crown Prince Hirohito's visit to Taiwan (April 17, 1923) File:Copper cattle of Taiwan Jinja.JPG|This copper bull was originally located at Taiwan Grand Shrine but was moved to in front of [[National Taiwan Museum]] after the end of World War II. </gallery>

== History == [[File:Taiwan-Grand-Shrine-Jingu-Map-1920s.png|thumb|300px|Taiwan Grand Shrine Map (1920s) records the location of each object and detailed information.]] Following the death of [[Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|Prince Yoshihisa]] in 1895, the [[Governor-General of Taiwan]] [[Nogi Maresuke]] began planning for a shrine in Yoshihisa's honor. Originally, the plan was to construct the shrine at Yuanshan Park ({{lang|zh-Hant|圓山公園}}, now part of [[Taipei Expo Park]]); however, Nogi's successor [[Kodama Gentarō]] and chief planner [[Gotō Shinpei]] decided to move it across the [[Keelung River]] to Jiantan Mountain ({{lang|zh-Hant|劍潭山}}) for the site's higher elevation. The vantage point would allow the shrine to overlook the entire city, making it symbolic for the Japanese Empire's colonial power.<ref name="lee">{{cite book |author=李東明 |title=永遠的北淡線 |publisher=玉山社 |isbn=957-8246-32-3 |pages=88–90 |language=zh-tw |chapter=宮之下 - 台灣神社下的火車站|year=2000 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |title=第四章 日治時期臺灣各級神社的選址與設域的空間特性 |pages=137–140 |url=http://rportal.lib.ntnu.edu.tw/bitstream/20.500.12235/94400/4/n088723001004.pdf |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |publisher=[[National Taiwan Normal University]] |language=zh-tw}}</ref> The construction lasted between 1900 and 1901, and the completed shrine was dedicated to Yoshihisa and the {{nihongo|Kaitaku Sanjin|開拓三神|Three Kami Deities of Pioneering}}.<ref name="hsu">{{cite web |author1=許書維 |title=臺灣神社誌 |url=https://www.th.gov.tw/epaper/site/page/78/1078 |website=國史館臺灣文獻館 |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw}}</ref> In 1915, a railway station named {{nihongo|Miyanoshita Station|宮ノ下乘降場||now [[Jiantan metro station]]}} was placed at the foot of Jiantan Mountain to serve the shrine.<ref name="lee"/> On April 12, 1923, [[Crown Prince]] [[Hirohito]], who would become Emperor Shōwa three years later, embarked on a two-week tour of Taiwan. In preparation for his visit to the shrine, {{nihongo|Chokushi Road|勅使街道|Chokushi kaidō|present-day Chungshan North Road}} was created leading up to the shrine from the city, with the {{nihongo|Meiji Bridge|明治橋}} crossing the Keelung River.<ref>{{cite web |author1=一流人 |title=我們住在「中國的地圖」裡？台北路名藏著滿滿的台灣過往 |url=https://www.gvm.com.tw/article/71003 |website=遠見 |publisher=天下文化 |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw |date=February 21, 2020}}</ref>
The shrine was elevated in rank to Grand Shrine (''jingū'') in 1944 ([[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] 19) when [[Amaterasu]] was enshrined, making it the highest-ranking shrine in Taiwan.<ref name="hsu"/> The opening ceremony of the new shrine was planned to be on October 28. However, on October 23, 1944, a cargo plane lost control and crashed atop the mountain where the Taiwan Grand Shrine was located, heavily damaging roughly half of the shrine.<ref>{{cite web |author1=郭俊麟 |title=淺談臺灣今昔地圖與歷史航照在中學地理資訊教育的意義 |url=http://epaper.ntu.edu.tw/view.php?id=10773 |website=GIS 教育推廣 電子報 |publisher=[[National Taiwan University]] |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw}}</ref> The shrine was never fully repaired due to [[Surrender of Japan|Japan's surrender after World War II]], and much of the shrine's materials were taken for construction projects elsewhere. In its place, the [[Grand Hotel (Taipei City)|Grand Hotel]] was constructed in 1952, where it remains as a prominent landmark of the city.<ref name="hsu"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Taiwan Grand Shrine.jpg|A [[postcard]] issued by the [[Governor-General of Taiwan]] of Taiwan Grand Shrine. The [[Imperial Seal of Japan|Imperial Seal]] is visible in the upper-right section. File:Taiwan_(Japanese_Colony)_1932_bank_note_-_10_yen_(front).jpg|Taiwan banknotes (10 yen banknotes) during the Japanese colonial era with the Taiwan Grand Shrine printed File:1923年日本皇太子裕仁訪拜臺灣神社_Japanese_Crown_Prince_Hirohito_on_the_way_to_Taiwan_Grand_Shrine.jpg|Prince Hirohito visits the Taiwan Grand Shrine during the Crown Prince Hirohito's visit to Taiwan (April 17, 1923) File:Copper cattle of Taiwan Jinja.JPG|This copper bull was originally located at Taiwan Grand Shrine but was moved to in front of [[National Taiwan Museum]] after the end of World War II. </gallery>
==See also== *[[Shintoism in Taiwan]] *[[List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan]]

At the bottom of the painting is the Keelung River and in the lower-right corner the Meiji Bridge is partially visible.]]

In preparation for his visit to the shrine, {{nihongo|Chokushi Road|勅使街道|Chokushi kaidō|present-day Chungshan North Road}} was created leading up to the shrine from the city, with the {{nihongo|Meiji Bridge|明治橋}} crossing the Keelung River.<ref>{{cite web |author1=一流人 |title=我們住在「中國的地圖」裡？台北路名藏著滿滿的台灣過往 |url=https://www.gvm.com.tw/article/71003 |website=遠見 |publisher=天下文化 |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw |date=February 21, 2020}}</ref>

However, on October 23, 1944, a cargo plane lost control and crashed atop the mountain where the Taiwan Grand Shrine was located, heavily damaging roughly half of the shrine.<ref>{{cite web |author1=郭俊麟 |title=淺談臺灣今昔地圖與歷史航照在中學地理資訊教育的意義 |url=http://epaper.ntu.edu.tw/view.php?id=10773 |website=GIS 教育推廣 電子報 |publisher=[[National Taiwan University]] |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw}}</ref> The shrine was never fully repaired due to [[Surrender of Japan|Japan's surrender after World War II]], and much of the shrine's materials were taken for construction projects elsewhere. It was located in [[Taipei|Taihoku]], [[Taiwan]] (now [[Zhongshan District, Taipei]]). Among the officially sanctioned Shinto shrines in Taiwan, Taiwan Grand Shrine held the highest rank of them all. The [[Grand Hotel (Taipei City)|Grand Hotel]] stands at the shrine's former site.
== History == [[File:Taiwan-Grand-Shrine-Jingu-Map-1920s.png|thumb|300px|Taiwan Grand Shrine Map (1920s) records the location of each object and detailed information.]] Following the death of [[Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|Prince Yoshihisa]] in 1895, the [[Governor-General of Taiwan]] [[Nogi Maresuke]] began planning for a shrine in Yoshihisa's honor. Originally, the plan was to construct the shrine at Yuanshan Park ({{lang|zh-Hant|圓山公園}}, now part of [[Taipei Expo Park]]); however, Nogi's successor [[Kodama Gentarō]] and chief planner [[Gotō Shinpei]] decided to move it across the [[Keelung River]] to Jiantan Mountain ({{lang|zh-Hant|劍潭山}}) for the site's higher elevation. The vantage point would allow the shrine to overlook the entire city, making it symbolic for the Japanese Empire's colonial power.<ref name="lee">{{cite book |author=李東明 |title=永遠的北淡線 |publisher=玉山社 |isbn=957-8246-32-3 |pages=88–90 |language=zh-tw |chapter=宮之下 - 台灣神社下的火車站|year=2000 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |title=第四章 日治時期臺灣各級神社的選址與設域的空間特性 |pages=137–140 |url=http://rportal.lib.ntnu.edu.tw/bitstream/20.500.12235/94400/4/n088723001004.pdf |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |publisher=[[National Taiwan Normal University]] |language=zh-tw}}</ref> The construction lasted between 1900 and 1901, and the completed shrine was dedicated to Yoshihisa and the {{nihongo|Kaitaku Sanjin|開拓三神|Three Kami Deities of Pioneering}}.<ref name="hsu">{{cite web |author1=許書維 |title=臺灣神社誌 |url=https://www.th.gov.tw/epaper/site/page/78/1078 |website=國史館臺灣文獻館 |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw}}</ref> In 1915, a railway station named {{nihongo|Miyanoshita Station|宮ノ下乘降場||now [[Jiantan metro station]]}} was placed at the foot of Jiantan Mountain to serve the shrine.<ref name="lee"/> On April 12, 1923, [[Crown Prince]] [[Hirohito]], who would become Emperor Shōwa three years later, embarked on a two-week tour of Taiwan. In preparation for his visit to the shrine, {{nihongo|Chokushi Road|勅使街道|Chokushi kaidō|present-day Chungshan North Road}} was created leading up to the shrine from the city, with the {{nihongo|Meiji Bridge|明治橋}} crossing the Keelung River.<ref>{{cite web |author1=一流人 |title=我們住在「中國的地圖」裡？台北路名藏著滿滿的台灣過往 |url=https://www.gvm.com.tw/article/71003 |website=遠見 |publisher=天下文化 |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw |date=February 21, 2020}}</ref>
The shrine was elevated in rank to Grand Shrine (''jingū'') in 1944 ([[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] 19) when [[Amaterasu]] was enshrined, making it the highest-ranking shrine in Taiwan.<ref name="hsu"/> The opening ceremony of the new shrine was planned to be on October 28. However, on October 23, 1944, a cargo plane lost control and crashed atop the mountain where the Taiwan Grand Shrine was located, heavily damaging roughly half of the shrine.<ref>{{cite web |author1=郭俊麟 |title=淺談臺灣今昔地圖與歷史航照在中學地理資訊教育的意義 |url=http://epaper.ntu.edu.tw/view.php?id=10773 |website=GIS 教育推廣 電子報 |publisher=[[National Taiwan University]] |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw}}</ref> The shrine was never fully repaired due to [[Surrender of Japan|Japan's surrender after World War II]], and much of the shrine's materials were taken for construction projects elsewhere. In its place, the [[Grand Hotel (Taipei City)|Grand Hotel]] was constructed in 1952, where it remains as a prominent landmark of the city.<ref name="hsu"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Taiwan Grand Shrine.jpg|A [[postcard]] issued by the [[Governor-General of Taiwan]] of Taiwan Grand Shrine. The [[Imperial Seal of Japan|Imperial Seal]] is visible in the upper-right section. File:Taiwan_(Japanese_Colony)_1932_bank_note_-_10_yen_(front).jpg|Taiwan banknotes (10 yen banknotes) during the Japanese colonial era with the Taiwan Grand Shrine printed File:1923年日本皇太子裕仁訪拜臺灣神社_Japanese_Crown_Prince_Hirohito_on_the_way_to_Taiwan_Grand_Shrine.jpg|Prince Hirohito visits the Taiwan Grand Shrine during the Crown Prince Hirohito's visit to Taiwan (April 17, 1923) File:Copper cattle of Taiwan Jinja.JPG|This copper bull was originally located at Taiwan Grand Shrine but was moved to in front of [[National Taiwan Museum]] after the end of World War II. </gallery>
==See also== *[[Shintoism in Taiwan]] *[[List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan]]

revision.text:
{{short description|Former Shinto shrine in Taipei, Taiwan}} [[File:Painting of Taiwan Grand Shrine.jpg|thumb|300px|A painting of Taiwan Grand Shrine during [[Taiwan under Japanese rule|Japanese colonial rule]]. At the bottom of the painting is the Keelung River and in the lower-right corner the Meiji Bridge is partially visible.]]
The '''Taiwan Grand Shrine''' ({{CJKV|c=臺灣神宮|r=Taiwan Jingū|p=Táiwān Shéngōng}}) was the highest ranking Japanese [[Shinto shrine]] in Taiwan during [[Taiwan under Japanese rule|Japanese colonial rule]]. It was located in [[Taipei|Taihoku]], [[Taiwan]] (now [[Zhongshan District, Taipei]]). Among the officially sanctioned Shinto shrines in Taiwan, Taiwan Grand Shrine held the highest rank of them all. The [[Grand Hotel (Taipei City)|Grand Hotel]] stands at the shrine's former site.
== History == [[File:Taiwan-Grand-Shrine-Jingu-Map-1920s.png|thumb|300px|Taiwan Grand Shrine Map (1920s) records the location of each object and detailed information.]] Following the death of [[Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|Prince Yoshihisa]] in 1895, the [[Governor-General of Taiwan]] [[Nogi Maresuke]] began planning for a shrine in Yoshihisa's honor. Originally, the plan was to construct the shrine at Yuanshan Park ({{lang|zh-Hant|圓山公園}}, now part of [[Taipei Expo Park]]); however, Nogi's successor [[Kodama Gentarō]] and chief planner [[Gotō Shinpei]] decided to move it across the [[Keelung River]] to Jiantan Mountain ({{lang|zh-Hant|劍潭山}}) for the site's higher elevation. The vantage point would allow the shrine to overlook the entire city, making it symbolic for the Japanese Empire's colonial power.<ref name="lee">{{cite book |author=李東明 |title=永遠的北淡線 |publisher=玉山社 |isbn=957-8246-32-3 |pages=88–90 |language=zh-tw |chapter=宮之下 - 台灣神社下的火車站|year=2000 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |title=第四章 日治時期臺灣各級神社的選址與設域的空間特性 |pages=137–140 |url=http://rportal.lib.ntnu.edu.tw/bitstream/20.500.12235/94400/4/n088723001004.pdf |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |publisher=[[National Taiwan Normal University]] |language=zh-tw}}</ref> The construction lasted between 1900 and 1901, and the completed shrine was dedicated to Yoshihisa and the {{nihongo|Kaitaku Sanjin|開拓三神|Three Kami Deities of Pioneering}}.<ref name="hsu">{{cite web |author1=許書維 |title=臺灣神社誌 |url=https://www.th.gov.tw/epaper/site/page/78/1078 |website=國史館臺灣文獻館 |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw}}</ref> In 1915, a railway station named {{nihongo|Miyanoshita Station|宮ノ下乘降場||now [[Jiantan metro station]]}} was placed at the foot of Jiantan Mountain to serve the shrine.<ref name="lee"/> On April 12, 1923, [[Crown Prince]] [[Hirohito]], who would become Emperor Shōwa three years later, embarked on a two-week tour of Taiwan. In preparation for his visit to the shrine, {{nihongo|Chokushi Road|勅使街道|Chokushi kaidō|present-day Chungshan North Road}} was created leading up to the shrine from the city, with the {{nihongo|Meiji Bridge|明治橋}} crossing the Keelung River.<ref>{{cite web |author1=一流人 |title=我們住在「中國的地圖」裡？台北路名藏著滿滿的台灣過往 |url=https://www.gvm.com.tw/article/71003 |website=遠見 |publisher=天下文化 |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw |date=February 21, 2020}}</ref>
The shrine was elevated in rank to Grand Shrine (''jingū'') in 1944 ([[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] 19) when [[Amaterasu]] was enshrined, making it the highest-ranking shrine in Taiwan.<ref name="hsu"/> The opening ceremony of the new shrine was planned to be on October 28. However, on October 23, 1944, a cargo plane lost control and crashed atop the mountain where the Taiwan Grand Shrine was located, heavily damaging roughly half of the shrine.<ref>{{cite web |author1=郭俊麟 |title=淺談臺灣今昔地圖與歷史航照在中學地理資訊教育的意義 |url=http://epaper.ntu.edu.tw/view.php?id=10773 |website=GIS 教育推廣 電子報 |publisher=[[National Taiwan University]] |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw}}</ref> The shrine was never fully repaired due to [[Surrender of Japan|Japan's surrender after World War II]], and much of the shrine's materials were taken for construction projects elsewhere. In its place, the [[Grand Hotel (Taipei City)|Grand Hotel]] was constructed in 1952, where it remains as a prominent landmark of the city.<ref name="hsu"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Taiwan Grand Shrine.jpg|A [[postcard]] issued by the [[Governor-General of Taiwan]] of Taiwan Grand Shrine. The [[Imperial Seal of Japan|Imperial Seal]] is visible in the upper-right section. File:Taiwan_(Japanese_Colony)_1932_bank_note_-_10_yen_(front).jpg|Taiwan banknotes (10 yen banknotes) during the Japanese colonial era with the Taiwan Grand Shrine printed File:1923年日本皇太子裕仁訪拜臺灣神社_Japanese_Crown_Prince_Hirohito_on_the_way_to_Taiwan_Grand_Shrine.jpg|Prince Hirohito visits the Taiwan Grand Shrine during the Crown Prince Hirohito's visit to Taiwan (April 17, 1923) File:Copper cattle of Taiwan Jinja.JPG|This copper bull was originally located at Taiwan Grand Shrine but was moved to in front of [[National Taiwan Museum]] after the end of World War II. </gallery>
==See also== *[[Shintoism in Taiwan]] *[[List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan]]
==References== {{reflist}}
{{coord missing|Taiwan}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[category:kk]][[category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[en:Taiwan Grand Shrine]][[category:kk]][[category:Jingū]] [[category:kk]][[category:Shinto shrines in Taiwan]] [[category:kk]][[category:1901 establishments in Taiwan]] [[category:kk]][[category:1945 disestablishments in Taiwan]] [[category:kk]][[category:Taiwan under Japanese rule]] [[category:kk]][[category:20th-century Shinto shrines]] [[category:kk]][[category:Shinmei shrines]] [[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Taiwan Grand Shrine]]]] [[category:mm]] [[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Taiwan Grand Shrine]]]] [[category:mm]] [[en:Taiwan Grand Shrine]][[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Taiwan Grand Shrine]]]] [[category:mm]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Taiwan Grand Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[en:Taiwan Grand Shrine]][[en:Taiwan Grand Shrine]] {{Shinmei shrines}}

revision.comment: pre-cache bump
revision.text:
{{short description|Former Shinto shrine in Taipei, Taiwan}} [[File:Painting of Taiwan Grand Shrine.jpg|thumb|300px|A painting of Taiwan Grand Shrine during [[Taiwan under Japanese rule|Japanese colonial rule]]. At the bottom of the painting is the Keelung River and in the lower-right corner the Meiji Bridge is partially visible.]]
The '''Taiwan Grand Shrine''' ({{CJKV|c=臺灣神宮|r=Taiwan Jingū|p=Táiwān Shéngōng}}) was the highest ranking Japanese [[Shinto shrine]] in Taiwan during [[Taiwan under Japanese rule|Japanese colonial rule]]. It was located in [[Taipei|Taihoku]], [[Taiwan]] (now [[Zhongshan District, Taipei]]). Among the officially sanctioned Shinto shrines in Taiwan, Taiwan Grand Shrine held the highest rank of them all. The [[Grand Hotel (Taipei City)|Grand Hotel]] stands at the shrine's former site.
== History == [[File:Taiwan-Grand-Shrine-Jingu-Map-1920s.png|thumb|300px|Taiwan Grand Shrine Map (1920s) records the location of each object and detailed information.]] Following the death of [[Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|Prince Yoshihisa]] in 1895, the [[Governor-General of Taiwan]] [[Nogi Maresuke]] began planning for a shrine in Yoshihisa's honor. Originally, the plan was to construct the shrine at Yuanshan Park ({{lang|zh-Hant|圓山公園}}, now part of [[Taipei Expo Park]]); however, Nogi's successor [[Kodama Gentarō]] and chief planner [[Gotō Shinpei]] decided to move it across the [[Keelung River]] to Jiantan Mountain ({{lang|zh-Hant|劍潭山}}) for the site's higher elevation. The vantage point would allow the shrine to overlook the entire city, making it symbolic for the Japanese Empire's colonial power.<ref name="lee">{{cite book |author=李東明 |title=永遠的北淡線 |publisher=玉山社 |isbn=957-8246-32-3 |pages=88–90 |language=zh-tw |chapter=宮之下 - 台灣神社下的火車站|year=2000 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |title=第四章 日治時期臺灣各級神社的選址與設域的空間特性 |pages=137–140 |url=http://rportal.lib.ntnu.edu.tw/bitstream/20.500.12235/94400/4/n088723001004.pdf |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |publisher=[[National Taiwan Normal University]] |language=zh-tw}}</ref> The construction lasted between 1900 and 1901, and the completed shrine was dedicated to Yoshihisa and the {{nihongo|Kaitaku Sanjin|開拓三神|Three Kami Deities of Pioneering}}.<ref name="hsu">{{cite web |author1=許書維 |title=臺灣神社誌 |url=https://www.th.gov.tw/epaper/site/page/78/1078 |website=國史館臺灣文獻館 |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw}}</ref> In 1915, a railway station named {{nihongo|Miyanoshita Station|宮ノ下乘降場||now [[Jiantan metro station]]}} was placed at the foot of Jiantan Mountain to serve the shrine.<ref name="lee"/> On April 12, 1923, [[Crown Prince]] [[Hirohito]], who would become Emperor Shōwa three years later, embarked on a two-week tour of Taiwan. In preparation for his visit to the shrine, {{nihongo|Chokushi Road|勅使街道|Chokushi kaidō|present-day Chungshan North Road}} was created leading up to the shrine from the city, with the {{nihongo|Meiji Bridge|明治橋}} crossing the Keelung River.<ref>{{cite web |author1=一流人 |title=我們住在「中國的地圖」裡？台北路名藏著滿滿的台灣過往 |url=https://www.gvm.com.tw/article/71003 |website=遠見 |publisher=天下文化 |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw |date=February 21, 2020}}</ref>
The shrine was elevated in rank to Grand Shrine (''jingū'') in 1944 ([[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] 19) when [[Amaterasu]] was enshrined, making it the highest-ranking shrine in Taiwan.<ref name="hsu"/> The opening ceremony of the new shrine was planned to be on October 28. However, on October 23, 1944, a cargo plane lost control and crashed atop the mountain where the Taiwan Grand Shrine was located, heavily damaging roughly half of the shrine.<ref>{{cite web |author1=郭俊麟 |title=淺談臺灣今昔地圖與歷史航照在中學地理資訊教育的意義 |url=http://epaper.ntu.edu.tw/view.php?id=10773 |website=GIS 教育推廣 電子報 |publisher=[[National Taiwan University]] |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw}}</ref> The shrine was never fully repaired due to [[Surrender of Japan|Japan's surrender after World War II]], and much of the shrine's materials were taken for construction projects elsewhere. In its place, the [[Grand Hotel (Taipei City)|Grand Hotel]] was constructed in 1952, where it remains as a prominent landmark of the city.<ref name="hsu"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Taiwan Grand Shrine.jpg|A [[postcard]] issued by the [[Governor-General of Taiwan]] of Taiwan Grand Shrine. The [[Imperial Seal of Japan|Imperial Seal]] is visible in the upper-right section. File:Taiwan_(Japanese_Colony)_1932_bank_note_-_10_yen_(front).jpg|Taiwan banknotes (10 yen banknotes) during the Japanese colonial era with the Taiwan Grand Shrine printed File:1923年日本皇太子裕仁訪拜臺灣神社_Japanese_Crown_Prince_Hirohito_on_the_way_to_Taiwan_Grand_Shrine.jpg|Prince Hirohito visits the Taiwan Grand Shrine during the Crown Prince Hirohito's visit to Taiwan (April 17, 1923) File:Copper cattle of Taiwan Jinja.JPG|This copper bull was originally located at Taiwan Grand Shrine but was moved to in front of [[National Taiwan Museum]] after the end of World War II. </gallery>
==See also== *[[Shintoism in Taiwan]] *[[List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan]]
==References== {{reflist}}
{{coord missing|Taiwan}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Taiwan Grand Shrine]] [[category:kk]][[category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[en:Taiwan Grand Shrine]][[category:kk]][[category:Jingū]] [[category:kk]][[category:Shinto shrines in Taiwan]] [[category:kk]][[category:1901 establishments in Taiwan]] [[category:kk]][[category:1945 disestablishments in Taiwan]] [[category:kk]][[category:Taiwan under Japanese rule]] [[category:kk]][[category:20th-century Shinto shrines]] [[category:kk]][[category:Shinmei shrines]] [[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Taiwan Grand Shrine]]]] [[category:mm]] [[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Taiwan Grand Shrine]]]] [[category:mm]] [[en:Taiwan Grand Shrine]][[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Taiwan Grand Shrine]]]] [[category:mm]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Taiwan Grand Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[en:Taiwan Grand Shrine]][[en:Taiwan Grand Shrine]] {{Shinmei shrines}}

In its place, the [[Grand Hotel (Taipei City)|Grand Hotel]] was constructed in 1952, where it remains as a prominent landmark of the city.<ref name="hsu"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Taiwan Grand Shrine.jpg|A [[postcard]] issued by the [[Governor-General of Taiwan]] of Taiwan Grand Shrine. The [[Imperial Seal of Japan|Imperial Seal]] is visible in the upper-right section. File:Taiwan_(Japanese_Colony)_1932_bank_note_-_10_yen_(front).jpg|Taiwan banknotes (10 yen banknotes) during the Japanese colonial era with the Taiwan Grand Shrine printed File:1923年日本皇太子裕仁訪拜臺灣神社_Japanese_Crown_Prince_Hirohito_on_the_way_to_Taiwan_Grand_Shrine.jpg|Prince Hirohito visits the Taiwan Grand Shrine during the Crown Prince Hirohito's visit to Taiwan (April 17, 1923) File:Copper cattle of Taiwan Jinja.JPG|This copper bull was originally located at Taiwan Grand Shrine but was moved to in front of [[National Taiwan Museum]] after the end of World War II. </gallery>
==See also== *[[Shintoism in Taiwan]] *[[List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan]]

{{short description|Former Shinto shrine in Taipei, Taiwan}} [[File:Painting of Taiwan Grand Shrine.jpg|thumb|300px|A painting of Taiwan Grand Shrine during [[Taiwan under Japanese rule|Japanese colonial rule]]. At the bottom of the painting is the Keelung River and in the lower-right corner the Meiji Bridge is partially visible.]]
The '''Taiwan Grand Shrine''' ({{CJKV|c=臺灣神宮|r=Taiwan Jingū|p=Táiwān Shéngōng}}) was the highest ranking Japanese [[Shinto shrine]] in Taiwan during [[Taiwan under Japanese rule|Japanese colonial rule]]. It was located in [[Taipei|Taihoku]], [[Taiwan]] (now [[Zhongshan District, Taipei]]). Among the officially sanctioned Shinto shrines in Taiwan, Taiwan Grand Shrine held the highest rank of them all. The [[Grand Hotel (Taipei City)|Grand Hotel]] stands at the shrine's former site.
== History == [[File:Taiwan-Grand-Shrine-Jingu-Map-1920s.png|thumb|300px|Taiwan Grand Shrine Map (1920s) records the location of each object and detailed information.]] Following the death of [[Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|Prince Yoshihisa]] in 1895, the [[Governor-General of Taiwan]] [[Nogi Maresuke]] began planning for a shrine in Yoshihisa's honor. Originally, the plan was to construct the shrine at Yuanshan Park ({{lang|zh-Hant|圓山公園}}, now part of [[Taipei Expo Park]]); however, Nogi's successor [[Kodama Gentarō]] and chief planner [[Gotō Shinpei]] decided to move it across the [[Keelung River]] to Jiantan Mountain ({{lang|zh-Hant|劍潭山}}) for the site's higher elevation. The vantage point would allow the shrine to overlook the entire city, making it symbolic for the Japanese Empire's colonial power.<ref name="lee">{{cite book |author=李東明 |title=永遠的北淡線 |publisher=玉山社 |isbn=957-8246-32-3 |pages=88–90 |language=zh-tw |chapter=宮之下 - 台灣神社下的火車站|year=2000 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |title=第四章 日治時期臺灣各級神社的選址與設域的空間特性 |pages=137–140 |url=http://rportal.lib.ntnu.edu.tw/bitstream/20.500.12235/94400/4/n088723001004.pdf |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |publisher=[[National Taiwan Normal University]] |language=zh-tw}}</ref> The construction lasted between 1900 and 1901, and the completed shrine was dedicated to Yoshihisa and the {{nihongo|Kaitaku Sanjin|開拓三神|Three Kami Deities of Pioneering}}.<ref name="hsu">{{cite web |author1=許書維 |title=臺灣神社誌 |url=https://www.th.gov.tw/epaper/site/page/78/1078 |website=國史館臺灣文獻館 |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw}}</ref> In 1915, a railway station named {{nihongo|Miyanoshita Station|宮ノ下乘降場||now [[Jiantan metro station]]}} was placed at the foot of Jiantan Mountain to serve the shrine.<ref name="lee"/> On April 12, 1923, [[Crown Prince]] [[Hirohito]], who would become Emperor Shōwa three years later, embarked on a two-week tour of Taiwan. In preparation for his visit to the shrine, {{nihongo|Chokushi Road|勅使街道|Chokushi kaidō|present-day Chungshan North Road}} was created leading up to the shrine from the city, with the {{nihongo|Meiji Bridge|明治橋}} crossing the Keelung River.<ref>{{cite web |author1=一流人 |title=我們住在「中國的地圖」裡？台北路名藏著滿滿的台灣過往 |url=https://www.gvm.com.tw/article/71003 |website=遠見 |publisher=天下文化 |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw |date=February 21, 2020}}</ref>
The shrine was elevated in rank to Grand Shrine (''jingū'') in 1944 ([[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] 19) when [[Amaterasu]] was enshrined, making it the highest-ranking shrine in Taiwan.<ref name="hsu"/> The opening ceremony of the new shrine was planned to be on October 28. However, on October 23, 1944, a cargo plane lost control and crashed atop the mountain where the Taiwan Grand Shrine was located, heavily damaging roughly half of the shrine.<ref>{{cite web |author1=郭俊麟 |title=淺談臺灣今昔地圖與歷史航照在中學地理資訊教育的意義 |url=http://epaper.ntu.edu.tw/view.php?id=10773 |website=GIS 教育推廣 電子報 |publisher=[[National Taiwan University]] |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw}}</ref> The shrine was never fully repaired due to [[Surrender of Japan|Japan's surrender after World War II]], and much of the shrine's materials were taken for construction projects elsewhere. In its place, the [[Grand Hotel (Taipei City)|Grand Hotel]] was constructed in 1952, where it remains as a prominent landmark of the city.<ref name="hsu"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Taiwan Grand Shrine.jpg|A [[postcard]] issued by the [[Governor-General of Taiwan]] of Taiwan Grand Shrine. The [[Imperial Seal of Japan|Imperial Seal]] is visible in the upper-right section. File:Taiwan_(Japanese_Colony)_1932_bank_note_-_10_yen_(front).jpg|Taiwan banknotes (10 yen banknotes) during the Japanese colonial era with the Taiwan Grand Shrine printed File:1923年日本皇太子裕仁訪拜臺灣神社_Japanese_Crown_Prince_Hirohito_on_the_way_to_Taiwan_Grand_Shrine.jpg|Prince Hirohito visits the Taiwan Grand Shrine during the Crown Prince Hirohito's visit to Taiwan (April 17, 1923) File:Copper cattle of Taiwan Jinja.JPG|This copper bull was originally located at Taiwan Grand Shrine but was moved to in front of [[National Taiwan Museum]] after the end of World War II. </gallery>

Originally, the plan was to construct the shrine at Yuanshan Park ({{lang|zh-Hant|圓山公園}}, now part of [[Taipei Expo Park]]); however, Nogi's successor [[Kodama Gentarō]] and chief planner [[Gotō Shinpei]] decided to move it across the [[Keelung River]] to Jiantan Mountain ({{lang|zh-Hant|劍潭山}}) for the site's higher elevation. The vantage point would allow the shrine to overlook the entire city, making it symbolic for the Japanese Empire's colonial power.<ref name="lee">{{cite book |author=李東明 |title=永遠的北淡線 |publisher=玉山社 |isbn=957-8246-32-3 |pages=88–90 |language=zh-tw |chapter=宮之下 - 台灣神社下的火車站|year=2000 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |title=第四章 日治時期臺灣各級神社的選址與設域的空間特性 |pages=137–140 |url=http://rportal.lib.ntnu.edu.tw/bitstream/20.500.12235/94400/4/n088723001004.pdf |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |publisher=[[National Taiwan Normal University]] |language=zh-tw}}</ref> The construction lasted between 1900 and 1901, and the completed shrine was dedicated to Yoshihisa and the {{nihongo|Kaitaku Sanjin|開拓三神|Three Kami Deities of Pioneering}}.<ref name="hsu">{{cite web |author1=許書維 |title=臺灣神社誌 |url=https://www.th.gov.tw/epaper/site/page/78/1078 |website=國史館臺灣文獻館 |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw}}</ref> In 1915, a railway station named {{nihongo|Miyanoshita Station|宮ノ下乘降場||now [[Jiantan metro station]]}} was placed at the foot of Jiantan Mountain to serve the shrine.<ref name="lee"/> On April 12, 1923, [[Crown Prince]] [[Hirohito]], who would become Emperor Shōwa three years later, embarked on a two-week tour of Taiwan. In preparation for his visit to the shrine, {{nihongo|Chokushi Road|勅使街道|Chokushi kaidō|present-day Chungshan North Road}} was created leading up to the shrine from the city, with the {{nihongo|Meiji Bridge|明治橋}} crossing the Keelung River.<ref>{{cite web |author1=一流人 |title=我們住在「中國的地圖」裡？台北路名藏著滿滿的台灣過往 |url=https://www.gvm.com.tw/article/71003 |website=遠見 |publisher=天下文化 |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw |date=February 21, 2020}}</ref>
The shrine was elevated in rank to Grand Shrine (''jingū'') in 1944 ([[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] 19) when [[Amaterasu]] was enshrined, making it the highest-ranking shrine in Taiwan.<ref name="hsu"/> The opening ceremony of the new shrine was planned to be on October 28. However, on October 23, 1944, a cargo plane lost control and crashed atop the mountain where the Taiwan Grand Shrine was located, heavily damaging roughly half of the shrine.<ref>{{cite web |author1=郭俊麟 |title=淺談臺灣今昔地圖與歷史航照在中學地理資訊教育的意義 |url=http://epaper.ntu.edu.tw/view.php?id=10773 |website=GIS 教育推廣 電子報 |publisher=[[National Taiwan University]] |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw}}</ref> The shrine was never fully repaired due to [[Surrender of Japan|Japan's surrender after World War II]], and much of the shrine's materials were taken for construction projects elsewhere. In its place, the [[Grand Hotel (Taipei City)|Grand Hotel]] was constructed in 1952, where it remains as a prominent landmark of the city.<ref name="hsu"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Taiwan Grand Shrine.jpg|A [[postcard]] issued by the [[Governor-General of Taiwan]] of Taiwan Grand Shrine. The [[Imperial Seal of Japan|Imperial Seal]] is visible in the upper-right section. File:Taiwan_(Japanese_Colony)_1932_bank_note_-_10_yen_(front).jpg|Taiwan banknotes (10 yen banknotes) during the Japanese colonial era with the Taiwan Grand Shrine printed File:1923年日本皇太子裕仁訪拜臺灣神社_Japanese_Crown_Prince_Hirohito_on_the_way_to_Taiwan_Grand_Shrine.jpg|Prince Hirohito visits the Taiwan Grand Shrine during the Crown Prince Hirohito's visit to Taiwan (April 17, 1923) File:Copper cattle of Taiwan Jinja.JPG|This copper bull was originally located at Taiwan Grand Shrine but was moved to in front of [[National Taiwan Museum]] after the end of World War II. </gallery>
==See also== *[[Shintoism in Taiwan]] *[[List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan]]

{{short description|Former Shinto shrine in Taipei, Taiwan}} [[File:Painting of Taiwan Grand Shrine.jpg|thumb|300px|A painting of Taiwan Grand Shrine during [[Taiwan under Japanese rule|Japanese colonial rule]]. At the bottom of the painting is the Keelung River and in the lower-right corner the Meiji Bridge is partially visible.]]
The '''Taiwan Grand Shrine''' ({{CJKV|c=臺灣神宮|r=Taiwan Jingū|p=Táiwān Shéngōng}}) was the highest ranking Japanese [[Shinto shrine]] in Taiwan during [[Taiwan under Japanese rule|Japanese colonial rule]]. It was located in [[Taipei|Taihoku]], [[Taiwan]] (now [[Zhongshan District, Taipei]]). Among the officially sanctioned Shinto shrines in Taiwan, Taiwan Grand Shrine held the highest rank of them all. The [[Grand Hotel (Taipei City)|Grand Hotel]] stands at the shrine's former site.
== History == [[File:Taiwan-Grand-Shrine-Jingu-Map-1920s.png|thumb|300px|Taiwan Grand Shrine Map (1920s) records the location of each object and detailed information.]] Following the death of [[Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|Prince Yoshihisa]] in 1895, the [[Governor-General of Taiwan]] [[Nogi Maresuke]] began planning for a shrine in Yoshihisa's honor. Originally, the plan was to construct the shrine at Yuanshan Park ({{lang|zh-Hant|圓山公園}}, now part of [[Taipei Expo Park]]); however, Nogi's successor [[Kodama Gentarō]] and chief planner [[Gotō Shinpei]] decided to move it across the [[Keelung River]] to Jiantan Mountain ({{lang|zh-Hant|劍潭山}}) for the site's higher elevation. The vantage point would allow the shrine to overlook the entire city, making it symbolic for the Japanese Empire's colonial power.<ref name="lee">{{cite book |author=李東明 |title=永遠的北淡線 |publisher=玉山社 |isbn=957-8246-32-3 |pages=88–90 |language=zh-tw |chapter=宮之下 - 台灣神社下的火車站|year=2000 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |title=第四章 日治時期臺灣各級神社的選址與設域的空間特性 |pages=137–140 |url=http://rportal.lib.ntnu.edu.tw/bitstream/20.500.12235/94400/4/n088723001004.pdf |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |publisher=[[National Taiwan Normal University]] |language=zh-tw}}</ref> The construction lasted between 1900 and 1901, and the completed shrine was dedicated to Yoshihisa and the {{nihongo|Kaitaku Sanjin|開拓三神|Three Kami Deities of Pioneering}}.<ref name="hsu">{{cite web |author1=許書維 |title=臺灣神社誌 |url=https://www.th.gov.tw/epaper/site/page/78/1078 |website=國史館臺灣文獻館 |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw}}</ref> In 1915, a railway station named {{nihongo|Miyanoshita Station|宮ノ下乘降場||now [[Jiantan metro station]]}} was placed at the foot of Jiantan Mountain to serve the shrine.<ref name="lee"/> On April 12, 1923, [[Crown Prince]] [[Hirohito]], who would become Emperor Shōwa three years later, embarked on a two-week tour of Taiwan. In preparation for his visit to the shrine, {{nihongo|Chokushi Road|勅使街道|Chokushi kaidō|present-day Chungshan North Road}} was created leading up to the shrine from the city, with the {{nihongo|Meiji Bridge|明治橋}} crossing the Keelung River.<ref>{{cite web |author1=一流人 |title=我們住在「中國的地圖」裡？台北路名藏著滿滿的台灣過往 |url=https://www.gvm.com.tw/article/71003 |website=遠見 |publisher=天下文化 |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw |date=February 21, 2020}}</ref>
The shrine was elevated in rank to Grand Shrine (''jingū'') in 1944 ([[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] 19) when [[Amaterasu]] was enshrined, making it the highest-ranking shrine in Taiwan.<ref name="hsu"/> The opening ceremony of the new shrine was planned to be on October 28. However, on October 23, 1944, a cargo plane lost control and crashed atop the mountain where the Taiwan Grand Shrine was located, heavily damaging roughly half of the shrine.<ref>{{cite web |author1=郭俊麟 |title=淺談臺灣今昔地圖與歷史航照在中學地理資訊教育的意義 |url=http://epaper.ntu.edu.tw/view.php?id=10773 |website=GIS 教育推廣 電子報 |publisher=[[National Taiwan University]] |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw}}</ref> The shrine was never fully repaired due to [[Surrender of Japan|Japan's surrender after World War II]], and much of the shrine's materials were taken for construction projects elsewhere. In its place, the [[Grand Hotel (Taipei City)|Grand Hotel]] was constructed in 1952, where it remains as a prominent landmark of the city.<ref name="hsu"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Taiwan Grand Shrine.jpg|A [[postcard]] issued by the [[Governor-General of Taiwan]] of Taiwan Grand Shrine. The [[Imperial Seal of Japan|Imperial Seal]] is visible in the upper-right section. File:Taiwan_(Japanese_Colony)_1932_bank_note_-_10_yen_(front).jpg|Taiwan banknotes (10 yen banknotes) during the Japanese colonial era with the Taiwan Grand Shrine printed File:1923年日本皇太子裕仁訪拜臺灣神社_Japanese_Crown_Prince_Hirohito_on_the_way_to_Taiwan_Grand_Shrine.jpg|Prince Hirohito visits the Taiwan Grand Shrine during the Crown Prince Hirohito's visit to Taiwan (April 17, 1923) File:Copper cattle of Taiwan Jinja.JPG|This copper bull was originally located at Taiwan Grand Shrine but was moved to in front of [[National Taiwan Museum]] after the end of World War II. </gallery>
==See also== *[[Shintoism in Taiwan]] *[[List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan]]
==References== {{reflist}}

{{short description|Former Shinto shrine in Taipei, Taiwan}} [[File:Painting of Taiwan Grand Shrine.jpg|thumb|300px|A painting of Taiwan Grand Shrine during [[Taiwan under Japanese rule|Japanese colonial rule]]. At the bottom of the painting is the Keelung River and in the lower-right corner the Meiji Bridge is partially visible.]]
The '''Taiwan Grand Shrine''' ({{CJKV|c=臺灣神宮|r=Taiwan Jingū|p=Táiwān Shéngōng}}) was the highest ranking Japanese [[Shinto shrine]] in Taiwan during [[Taiwan under Japanese rule|Japanese colonial rule]]. It was located in [[Taipei|Taihoku]], [[Taiwan]] (now [[Zhongshan District, Taipei]]). Among the officially sanctioned Shinto shrines in Taiwan, Taiwan Grand Shrine held the highest rank of them all. The [[Grand Hotel (Taipei City)|Grand Hotel]] stands at the shrine's former site.
== History == [[File:Taiwan-Grand-Shrine-Jingu-Map-1920s.png|thumb|300px|Taiwan Grand Shrine Map (1920s) records the location of each object and detailed information.]] Following the death of [[Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|Prince Yoshihisa]] in 1895, the [[Governor-General of Taiwan]] [[Nogi Maresuke]] began planning for a shrine in Yoshihisa's honor. Originally, the plan was to construct the shrine at Yuanshan Park ({{lang|zh-Hant|圓山公園}}, now part of [[Taipei Expo Park]]); however, Nogi's successor [[Kodama Gentarō]] and chief planner [[Gotō Shinpei]] decided to move it across the [[Keelung River]] to Jiantan Mountain ({{lang|zh-Hant|劍潭山}}) for the site's higher elevation. The vantage point would allow the shrine to overlook the entire city, making it symbolic for the Japanese Empire's colonial power.<ref name="lee">{{cite book |author=李東明 |title=永遠的北淡線 |publisher=玉山社 |isbn=957-8246-32-3 |pages=88–90 |language=zh-tw |chapter=宮之下 - 台灣神社下的火車站|year=2000 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |title=第四章 日治時期臺灣各級神社的選址與設域的空間特性 |pages=137–140 |url=http://rportal.lib.ntnu.edu.tw/bitstream/20.500.12235/94400/4/n088723001004.pdf |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |publisher=[[National Taiwan Normal University]] |language=zh-tw}}</ref> The construction lasted between 1900 and 1901, and the completed shrine was dedicated to Yoshihisa and the {{nihongo|Kaitaku Sanjin|開拓三神|Three Kami Deities of Pioneering}}.<ref name="hsu">{{cite web |author1=許書維 |title=臺灣神社誌 |url=https://www.th.gov.tw/epaper/site/page/78/1078 |website=國史館臺灣文獻館 |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw}}</ref> In 1915, a railway station named {{nihongo|Miyanoshita Station|宮ノ下乘降場||now [[Jiantan metro station]]}} was placed at the foot of Jiantan Mountain to serve the shrine.<ref name="lee"/> On April 12, 1923, [[Crown Prince]] [[Hirohito]], who would become Emperor Shōwa three years later, embarked on a two-week tour of Taiwan. In preparation for his visit to the shrine, {{nihongo|Chokushi Road|勅使街道|Chokushi kaidō|present-day Chungshan North Road}} was created leading up to the shrine from the city, with the {{nihongo|Meiji Bridge|明治橋}} crossing the Keelung River.<ref>{{cite web |author1=一流人 |title=我們住在「中國的地圖」裡？台北路名藏著滿滿的台灣過往 |url=https://www.gvm.com.tw/article/71003 |website=遠見 |publisher=天下文化 |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw |date=February 21, 2020}}</ref>
The shrine was elevated in rank to Grand Shrine (''jingū'') in 1944 ([[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] 19) when [[Amaterasu]] was enshrined, making it the highest-ranking shrine in Taiwan.<ref name="hsu"/> The opening ceremony of the new shrine was planned to be on October 28. However, on October 23, 1944, a cargo plane lost control and crashed atop the mountain where the Taiwan Grand Shrine was located, heavily damaging roughly half of the shrine.<ref>{{cite web |author1=郭俊麟 |title=淺談臺灣今昔地圖與歷史航照在中學地理資訊教育的意義 |url=http://epaper.ntu.edu.tw/view.php?id=10773 |website=GIS 教育推廣 電子報 |publisher=[[National Taiwan University]] |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw}}</ref> The shrine was never fully repaired due to [[Surrender of Japan|Japan's surrender after World War II]], and much of the shrine's materials were taken for construction projects elsewhere. In its place, the [[Grand Hotel (Taipei City)|Grand Hotel]] was constructed in 1952, where it remains as a prominent landmark of the city.<ref name="hsu"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Taiwan Grand Shrine.jpg|A [[postcard]] issued by the [[Governor-General of Taiwan]] of Taiwan Grand Shrine. The [[Imperial Seal of Japan|Imperial Seal]] is visible in the upper-right section. File:Taiwan_(Japanese_Colony)_1932_bank_note_-_10_yen_(front).jpg|Taiwan banknotes (10 yen banknotes) during the Japanese colonial era with the Taiwan Grand Shrine printed File:1923年日本皇太子裕仁訪拜臺灣神社_Japanese_Crown_Prince_Hirohito_on_the_way_to_Taiwan_Grand_Shrine.jpg|Prince Hirohito visits the Taiwan Grand Shrine during the Crown Prince Hirohito's visit to Taiwan (April 17, 1923) File:Copper cattle of Taiwan Jinja.JPG|This copper bull was originally located at Taiwan Grand Shrine but was moved to in front of [[National Taiwan Museum]] after the end of World War II. </gallery>
==See also== *[[Shintoism in Taiwan]] *[[List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan]]

The vantage point would allow the shrine to overlook the entire city, making it symbolic for the Japanese Empire's colonial power.<ref name="lee">{{cite book |author=李東明 |title=永遠的北淡線 |publisher=玉山社 |isbn=957-8246-32-3 |pages=88–90 |language=zh-tw |chapter=宮之下 - 台灣神社下的火車站|year=2000 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |title=第四章 日治時期臺灣各級神社的選址與設域的空間特性 |pages=137–140 |url=http://rportal.lib.ntnu.edu.tw/bitstream/20.500.12235/94400/4/n088723001004.pdf |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |publisher=[[National Taiwan Normal University]] |language=zh-tw}}</ref> The construction lasted between 1900 and 1901, and the completed shrine was dedicated to Yoshihisa and the {{nihongo|Kaitaku Sanjin|開拓三神|Three Kami Deities of Pioneering}}.<ref name="hsu">{{cite web |author1=許書維 |title=臺灣神社誌 |url=https://www.th.gov.tw/epaper/site/page/78/1078 |website=國史館臺灣文獻館 |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw}}</ref> In 1915, a railway station named {{nihongo|Miyanoshita Station|宮ノ下乘降場||now [[Jiantan metro station]]}} was placed at the foot of Jiantan Mountain to serve the shrine.<ref name="lee"/> On April 12, 1923, [[Crown Prince]] [[Hirohito]], who would become Emperor Shōwa three years later, embarked on a two-week tour of Taiwan. The shrine was elevated in rank to Grand Shrine (''jingū'') in 1944 ([[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] 19) when [[Amaterasu]] was enshrined, making it the highest-ranking shrine in Taiwan.<ref name="hsu"/> The opening ceremony of the new shrine was planned to be on October 28. However, on October 23, 1944, a cargo plane lost control and crashed atop the mountain where the Taiwan Grand Shrine was located, heavily damaging roughly half of the shrine.<ref>{{cite web |author1=郭俊麟 |title=淺談臺灣今昔地圖與歷史航照在中學地理資訊教育的意義 |url=http://epaper.ntu.edu.tw/view.php?id=10773 |website=GIS 教育推廣 電子報 |publisher=[[National Taiwan University]] |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw}}</ref> The shrine was never fully repaired due to [[Surrender of Japan|Japan's surrender after World War II]], and much of the shrine's materials were taken for construction projects elsewhere. In its place, the [[Grand Hotel (Taipei City)|Grand Hotel]] was constructed in 1952, where it remains as a prominent landmark of the city.<ref name="hsu"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Taiwan Grand Shrine.jpg|A [[postcard]] issued by the [[Governor-General of Taiwan]] of Taiwan Grand Shrine. The [[Imperial Seal of Japan|Imperial Seal]] is visible in the upper-right section. File:Taiwan_(Japanese_Colony)_1932_bank_note_-_10_yen_(front).jpg|Taiwan banknotes (10 yen banknotes) during the Japanese colonial era with the Taiwan Grand Shrine printed File:1923年日本皇太子裕仁訪拜臺灣神社_Japanese_Crown_Prince_Hirohito_on_the_way_to_Taiwan_Grand_Shrine.jpg|Prince Hirohito visits the Taiwan Grand Shrine during the Crown Prince Hirohito's visit to Taiwan (April 17, 1923) File:Copper cattle of Taiwan Jinja.JPG|This copper bull was originally located at Taiwan Grand Shrine but was moved to in front of [[National Taiwan Museum]] after the end of World War II. </gallery>
==See also== *[[Shintoism in Taiwan]] *[[List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan]]

revision.text:
{{short description|Former Shinto shrine in Taipei, Taiwan}} [[File:Painting of Taiwan Grand Shrine.jpg|thumb|300px|A painting of Taiwan Grand Shrine during [[Taiwan under Japanese rule|Japanese colonial rule]]. At the bottom of the painting is the Keelung River and in the lower-right corner the Meiji Bridge is partially visible.]]
The '''Taiwan Grand Shrine''' ({{CJKV|c=臺灣神宮|r=Taiwan Jingū|p=Táiwān Shéngōng}}) was the highest ranking Japanese [[Shinto shrine]] in Taiwan during [[Taiwan under Japanese rule|Japanese colonial rule]]. It was located in [[Taipei|Taihoku]], [[Taiwan]] (now [[Zhongshan District, Taipei]]). Among the officially sanctioned Shinto shrines in Taiwan, Taiwan Grand Shrine held the highest rank of them all. The [[Grand Hotel (Taipei City)|Grand Hotel]] stands at the shrine's former site.
== History == [[File:Taiwan-Grand-Shrine-Jingu-Map-1920s.png|thumb|300px|Taiwan Grand Shrine Map (1920s) records the location of each object and detailed information.]] Following the death of [[Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|Prince Yoshihisa]] in 1895, the [[Governor-General of Taiwan]] [[Nogi Maresuke]] began planning for a shrine in Yoshihisa's honor. Originally, the plan was to construct the shrine at Yuanshan Park ({{lang|zh-Hant|圓山公園}}, now part of [[Taipei Expo Park]]); however, Nogi's successor [[Kodama Gentarō]] and chief planner [[Gotō Shinpei]] decided to move it across the [[Keelung River]] to Jiantan Mountain ({{lang|zh-Hant|劍潭山}}) for the site's higher elevation. The vantage point would allow the shrine to overlook the entire city, making it symbolic for the Japanese Empire's colonial power.<ref name="lee">{{cite book |author=李東明 |title=永遠的北淡線 |publisher=玉山社 |isbn=957-8246-32-3 |pages=88–90 |language=zh-tw |chapter=宮之下 - 台灣神社下的火車站|year=2000 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |title=第四章 日治時期臺灣各級神社的選址與設域的空間特性 |pages=137–140 |url=http://rportal.lib.ntnu.edu.tw/bitstream/20.500.12235/94400/4/n088723001004.pdf |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |publisher=[[National Taiwan Normal University]] |language=zh-tw}}</ref> The construction lasted between 1900 and 1901, and the completed shrine was dedicated to Yoshihisa and the {{nihongo|Kaitaku Sanjin|開拓三神|Three Kami Deities of Pioneering}}.<ref name="hsu">{{cite web |author1=許書維 |title=臺灣神社誌 |url=https://www.th.gov.tw/epaper/site/page/78/1078 |website=國史館臺灣文獻館 |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw}}</ref> In 1915, a railway station named {{nihongo|Miyanoshita Station|宮ノ下乘降場||now [[Jiantan metro station]]}} was placed at the foot of Jiantan Mountain to serve the shrine.<ref name="lee"/> On April 12, 1923, [[Crown Prince]] [[Hirohito]], who would become Emperor Shōwa three years later, embarked on a two-week tour of Taiwan. In preparation for his visit to the shrine, {{nihongo|Chokushi Road|勅使街道|Chokushi kaidō|present-day Chungshan North Road}} was created leading up to the shrine from the city, with the {{nihongo|Meiji Bridge|明治橋}} crossing the Keelung River.<ref>{{cite web |author1=一流人 |title=我們住在「中國的地圖」裡？台北路名藏著滿滿的台灣過往 |url=https://www.gvm.com.tw/article/71003 |website=遠見 |publisher=天下文化 |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw |date=February 21, 2020}}</ref>
The shrine was elevated in rank to Grand Shrine (''jingū'') in 1944 ([[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] 19) when [[Amaterasu]] was enshrined, making it the highest-ranking shrine in Taiwan.<ref name="hsu"/> The opening ceremony of the new shrine was planned to be on October 28. However, on October 23, 1944, a cargo plane lost control and crashed atop the mountain where the Taiwan Grand Shrine was located, heavily damaging roughly half of the shrine.<ref>{{cite web |author1=郭俊麟 |title=淺談臺灣今昔地圖與歷史航照在中學地理資訊教育的意義 |url=http://epaper.ntu.edu.tw/view.php?id=10773 |website=GIS 教育推廣 電子報 |publisher=[[National Taiwan University]] |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw}}</ref> The shrine was never fully repaired due to [[Surrender of Japan|Japan's surrender after World War II]], and much of the shrine's materials were taken for construction projects elsewhere. In its place, the [[Grand Hotel (Taipei City)|Grand Hotel]] was constructed in 1952, where it remains as a prominent landmark of the city.<ref name="hsu"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Taiwan Grand Shrine.jpg|A [[postcard]] issued by the [[Governor-General of Taiwan]] of Taiwan Grand Shrine. The [[Imperial Seal of Japan|Imperial Seal]] is visible in the upper-right section. File:Taiwan_(Japanese_Colony)_1932_bank_note_-_10_yen_(front).jpg|Taiwan banknotes (10 yen banknotes) during the Japanese colonial era with the Taiwan Grand Shrine printed File:1923年日本皇太子裕仁訪拜臺灣神社_Japanese_Crown_Prince_Hirohito_on_the_way_to_Taiwan_Grand_Shrine.jpg|Prince Hirohito visits the Taiwan Grand Shrine during the Crown Prince Hirohito's visit to Taiwan (April 17, 1923) File:Copper cattle of Taiwan Jinja.JPG|This copper bull was originally located at Taiwan Grand Shrine but was moved to in front of [[National Taiwan Museum]] after the end of World War II. </gallery>
==See also== *[[Shintoism in Taiwan]] *[[List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan]]

revision.comment: added [[Category:Qq]]{{Beppyo Shrines}}[[Category:qq]] using [[Help:Gadget-HotCat|HotCat]]
revision.text:
{{short description|Former Shinto shrine in Taipei, Taiwan}} [[File:Painting of Taiwan Grand Shrine.jpg|thumb|300px|A painting of Taiwan Grand Shrine during [[Taiwan under Japanese rule|Japanese colonial rule]]. At the bottom of the painting is the Keelung River and in the lower-right corner the Meiji Bridge is partially visible.]]
The '''Taiwan Grand Shrine''' ({{CJKV|c=臺灣神宮|r=Taiwan Jingū|p=Táiwān Shéngōng}}) was the highest ranking Japanese [[Shinto shrine]] in Taiwan during [[Taiwan under Japanese rule|Japanese colonial rule]]. It was located in [[Taipei|Taihoku]], [[Taiwan]] (now [[Zhongshan District, Taipei]]). Among the officially sanctioned Shinto shrines in Taiwan, Taiwan Grand Shrine held the highest rank of them all. The [[Grand Hotel (Taipei City)|Grand Hotel]] stands at the shrine's former site.
== History == [[File:Taiwan-Grand-Shrine-Jingu-Map-1920s.png|thumb|300px|Taiwan Grand Shrine Map (1920s) records the location of each object and detailed information.]] Following the death of [[Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|Prince Yoshihisa]] in 1895, the [[Governor-General of Taiwan]] [[Nogi Maresuke]] began planning for a shrine in Yoshihisa's honor. Originally, the plan was to construct the shrine at Yuanshan Park ({{lang|zh-Hant|圓山公園}}, now part of [[Taipei Expo Park]]); however, Nogi's successor [[Kodama Gentarō]] and chief planner [[Gotō Shinpei]] decided to move it across the [[Keelung River]] to Jiantan Mountain ({{lang|zh-Hant|劍潭山}}) for the site's higher elevation. The vantage point would allow the shrine to overlook the entire city, making it symbolic for the Japanese Empire's colonial power.<ref name="lee">{{cite book |author=李東明 |title=永遠的北淡線 |publisher=玉山社 |isbn=957-8246-32-3 |pages=88–90 |language=zh-tw |chapter=宮之下 - 台灣神社下的火車站|year=2000 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |title=第四章 日治時期臺灣各級神社的選址與設域的空間特性 |pages=137–140 |url=http://rportal.lib.ntnu.edu.tw/bitstream/20.500.12235/94400/4/n088723001004.pdf |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |publisher=[[National Taiwan Normal University]] |language=zh-tw}}</ref> The construction lasted between 1900 and 1901, and the completed shrine was dedicated to Yoshihisa and the {{nihongo|Kaitaku Sanjin|開拓三神|Three Kami Deities of Pioneering}}.<ref name="hsu">{{cite web |author1=許書維 |title=臺灣神社誌 |url=https://www.th.gov.tw/epaper/site/page/78/1078 |website=國史館臺灣文獻館 |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw}}</ref> In 1915, a railway station named {{nihongo|Miyanoshita Station|宮ノ下乘降場||now [[Jiantan metro station]]}} was placed at the foot of Jiantan Mountain to serve the shrine.<ref name="lee"/> On April 12, 1923, [[Crown Prince]] [[Hirohito]], who would become Emperor Shōwa three years later, embarked on a two-week tour of Taiwan. In preparation for his visit to the shrine, {{nihongo|Chokushi Road|勅使街道|Chokushi kaidō|present-day Chungshan North Road}} was created leading up to the shrine from the city, with the {{nihongo|Meiji Bridge|明治橋}} crossing the Keelung River.<ref>{{cite web |author1=一流人 |title=我們住在「中國的地圖」裡？台北路名藏著滿滿的台灣過往 |url=https://www.gvm.com.tw/article/71003 |website=遠見 |publisher=天下文化 |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw |date=February 21, 2020}}</ref>
The shrine was elevated in rank to Grand Shrine (''jingū'') in 1944 ([[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] 19) when [[Amaterasu]] was enshrined, making it the highest-ranking shrine in Taiwan.<ref name="hsu"/> The opening ceremony of the new shrine was planned to be on October 28. However, on October 23, 1944, a cargo plane lost control and crashed atop the mountain where the Taiwan Grand Shrine was located, heavily damaging roughly half of the shrine.<ref>{{cite web |author1=郭俊麟 |title=淺談臺灣今昔地圖與歷史航照在中學地理資訊教育的意義 |url=http://epaper.ntu.edu.tw/view.php?id=10773 |website=GIS 教育推廣 電子報 |publisher=[[National Taiwan University]] |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw}}</ref> The shrine was never fully repaired due to [[Surrender of Japan|Japan's surrender after World War II]], and much of the shrine's materials were taken for construction projects elsewhere. In its place, the [[Grand Hotel (Taipei City)|Grand Hotel]] was constructed in 1952, where it remains as a prominent landmark of the city.<ref name="hsu"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Taiwan Grand Shrine.jpg|A [[postcard]] issued by the [[Governor-General of Taiwan]] of Taiwan Grand Shrine. The [[Imperial Seal of Japan|Imperial Seal]] is visible in the upper-right section. File:Taiwan_(Japanese_Colony)_1932_bank_note_-_10_yen_(front).jpg|Taiwan banknotes (10 yen banknotes) during the Japanese colonial era with the Taiwan Grand Shrine printed File:1923年日本皇太子裕仁訪拜臺灣神社_Japanese_Crown_Prince_Hirohito_on_the_way_to_Taiwan_Grand_Shrine.jpg|Prince Hirohito visits the Taiwan Grand Shrine during the Crown Prince Hirohito's visit to Taiwan (April 17, 1923) File:Copper cattle of Taiwan Jinja.JPG|This copper bull was originally located at Taiwan Grand Shrine but was moved to in front of [[National Taiwan Museum]] after the end of World War II. </gallery>
==See also== *[[Shintoism in Taiwan]] *[[List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan]]
