At the bottom of the painting is the Keelung River and in the lower-right corner the Meiji Bridge is partially visible.]]
The '''Taiwan Grand Shrine''' ({{CJKV|c=臺灣神宮|r=Taiwan Jingū|p=Táiwān Shéngōng}}) was the highest Ranking Japanese [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in Taiwan during [[:en:Taiwan under Japanese rule|Japanese colonial rule]]. It was located in [[:en:Taipei|Taihoku]], [[:en:Taiwan|Taiwan]] (now [[:en:Zhongshan District, Taipei|Zhongshan District, Taipei]]). Among the officially sanctioned Shinto shrines in Taiwan, Taiwan Grand Shrine held the highest Rank of them all. The [[:en:Grand Hotel (Taipei City)|Grand Hotel]] stands at the shrine's former site.
== History == [[File:Taiwan-Grand-Shrine-Jingu-Map-1920s.png|thumb|300px|Taiwan Grand Shrine Map (1920s) records the location of each object and detailed information.]] Following the death of [[:en:Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|Prince Yoshihisa]] in 1895, the [[:en:Governor-General of Taiwan|Governor-General of Taiwan]] [[Nogi Maresuke]] began planning for a shrine in Yoshihisa's honor. Originally, the plan was to construct the shrine at Yuanshan Park ({{lang|zh-Hant|圓山公園}}, now part of [[:en:Taipei Expo Park|Taipei Expo Park]]); however, Nogi's successor [[:en:Kodama Gentarō|Kodama Gentarō]] and chief planner [[:en:Gotō Shinpei|Gotō Shinpei]] decided to move it across the [[:en:Keelung River|Keelung River]] to Jiantan Mountain ({{lang|zh-Hant|劍潭山}}) for the site's higher elevation. The vantage point would allow the shrine to overlook the entire city, making it symbolic for the Japanese Empire's colonial power.<ref name="lee">{{cite book |author=李東明 |title=永遠的北淡線 |publisher=玉山社 |isbn=957-8246-32-3 |pages=88–90 |language=zh-tw |chapter=宮之下 - 台灣神社下的火車站|year=2000 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |title=第四章 日治時期臺灣各級神社的選址與設域的空間特性 |pages=137–140 |url=http://rportal.lib.ntnu.edu.tw/bitstream/20.500.12235/94400/4/n088723001004.pdf |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |publisher=[[National Taiwan Normal University]] |language=zh-tw}}</ref> The construction lasted between 1900 and 1901, and the completed shrine was dedicated to Yoshihisa and the {{nihongo|Kaitaku Sanjin|開拓三神|Three Kami Deities of Pioneering}}.<ref name="hsu">{{cite web |author1=許書維 |title=臺灣神社誌 |url=https://www.th.gov.tw/epaper/site/page/78/1078 |website=國史館臺灣文獻館 |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw}}</ref> In 1915, a railway station named {{nihongo|Miyanoshita Station|宮ノ下乘降場||now [[:en:Jiantan metro station|Jiantan metro station]]}} was placed at the foot of Jiantan Mountain to serve the shrine.<ref name="lee"/> On April 12, 1923, [[:en:Crown Prince|Crown Prince]] [[:en:Hirohito|Hirohito]], who would become Emperor Shōwa three years later, embarked on a two-week tour of Taiwan. In preparation for his visit to the shrine, {{nihongo|Chokushi Road|勅使街道|Chokushi kaidō|present-day Chungshan North Road}} was created leading up to the shrine from the city, with the {{nihongo|Meiji Bridge|明治橋}} crossing the Keelung River.<ref>{{cite web |author1=一流人 |title=我們住在「中國的地圖」裡？台北路名藏著滿滿的台灣過往 |url=https://www.gvm.com.tw/article/71003 |website=遠見 |publisher=天下文化 |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw |date=February 21, 2020}}</ref>
The shrine was elevated in Rank to Grand Shrine (''jingū'') in 1944 ([[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]] 19) when [[Amaterasu]] was enshrined, making it the highest-Ranking shrine in Taiwan.<ref name="hsu"/> The opening ceremony of the new shrine was planned to be on October 28. However, on October 23, 1944, a cargo plane lost control and crashed atop the mountain where the Taiwan Grand Shrine was located, heavily damaging roughly half of the shrine.<ref>{{cite web |author1=郭俊麟 |title=淺談臺灣今昔地圖與歷史航照在中學地理資訊教育的意義 |url=http://epaper.ntu.edu.tw/view.php?id=10773 |website=GIS 教育推廣 電子報 |publisher=[[National Taiwan University]] |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw}}</ref> The shrine was never fully repaired due to [[:en:Surrender of Japan|Japan's surrender after World War II]], and much of the shrine's materials were taken for construction projects elsewhere. In its place, the [[:en:Grand Hotel (Taipei City)|Grand Hotel]] was constructed in 1952, where it remains as a prominent landmark of the city.<ref name="hsu"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Taiwan Grand Shrine.jpg|A [[:en:Postcard|Postcard]] issued by the [[:en:Governor-General of Taiwan|Governor-General of Taiwan]] of Taiwan Grand Shrine. The [[:en:Imperial Seal of Japan|Imperial Seal]] is visible in the upper-right section. File:Taiwan_(Japanese_Colony)_1932_bank_note_-_10_yen_(front).jpg|Taiwan banknotes (10 yen banknotes) during the Japanese colonial era with the Taiwan Grand Shrine printed File:1923rd year 日本皇太子裕仁訪拜臺灣神社_Japanese_Crown_Prince_Hirohito_on_the_way_to_Taiwan_Grand_Shrine.jpg|Prince Hirohito visits the Taiwan Grand Shrine during the Crown Prince Hirohito's visit to Taiwan (April 17, 1923) File:Copper cattle of Taiwan Jinja.JPG|This copper bull was originally located at Taiwan Grand Shrine but was moved to in front of [[National Taiwan Museum]] after the end of World War II. </gallery>

The '''Taiwan Grand Shrine''' ({{CJKV|c=臺灣神宮|r=Taiwan Jingū|p=Táiwān Shéngōng}}) was the highest Ranking Japanese [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in Taiwan during [[:en:Taiwan under Japanese rule|Japanese colonial rule]]. It was located in [[:en:Taipei|Taihoku]], [[:en:Taiwan|Taiwan]] (now [[:en:Zhongshan District, Taipei|Zhongshan District, Taipei]]). Among the officially sanctioned Shinto shrines in Taiwan, Taiwan Grand Shrine held the highest Rank of them all. The [[:en:Grand Hotel (Taipei City)|Grand Hotel]] stands at the shrine's former site.
== History == [[File:Taiwan-Grand-Shrine-Jingu-Map-1920s.png|thumb|300px|Taiwan Grand Shrine Map (1920s) records the location of each object and detailed information.]] Following the death of [[:en:Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|Prince Yoshihisa]] in 1895, the [[:en:Governor-General of Taiwan|Governor-General of Taiwan]] [[Nogi Maresuke]] began planning for a shrine in Yoshihisa's honor. Originally, the plan was to construct the shrine at Yuanshan Park ({{lang|zh-Hant|圓山公園}}, now part of [[:en:Taipei Expo Park|Taipei Expo Park]]); however, Nogi's successor [[:en:Kodama Gentarō|Kodama Gentarō]] and chief planner [[:en:Gotō Shinpei|Gotō Shinpei]] decided to move it across the [[:en:Keelung River|Keelung River]] to Jiantan Mountain ({{lang|zh-Hant|劍潭山}}) for the site's higher elevation. The vantage point would allow the shrine to overlook the entire city, making it symbolic for the Japanese Empire's colonial power.<ref name="lee">{{cite book |author=李東明 |title=永遠的北淡線 |publisher=玉山社 |isbn=957-8246-32-3 |pages=88–90 |language=zh-tw |chapter=宮之下 - 台灣神社下的火車站|year=2000 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |title=第四章 日治時期臺灣各級神社的選址與設域的空間特性 |pages=137–140 |url=http://rportal.lib.ntnu.edu.tw/bitstream/20.500.12235/94400/4/n088723001004.pdf |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |publisher=[[National Taiwan Normal University]] |language=zh-tw}}</ref> The construction lasted between 1900 and 1901, and the completed shrine was dedicated to Yoshihisa and the {{nihongo|Kaitaku Sanjin|開拓三神|Three Kami Deities of Pioneering}}.<ref name="hsu">{{cite web |author1=許書維 |title=臺灣神社誌 |url=https://www.th.gov.tw/epaper/site/page/78/1078 |website=國史館臺灣文獻館 |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw}}</ref> In 1915, a railway station named {{nihongo|Miyanoshita Station|宮ノ下乘降場||now [[:en:Jiantan metro station|Jiantan metro station]]}} was placed at the foot of Jiantan Mountain to serve the shrine.<ref name="lee"/> On April 12, 1923, [[:en:Crown Prince|Crown Prince]] [[:en:Hirohito|Hirohito]], who would become Emperor Shōwa three years later, embarked on a two-week tour of Taiwan. In preparation for his visit to the shrine, {{nihongo|Chokushi Road|勅使街道|Chokushi kaidō|present-day Chungshan North Road}} was created leading up to the shrine from the city, with the {{nihongo|Meiji Bridge|明治橋}} crossing the Keelung River.<ref>{{cite web |author1=一流人 |title=我們住在「中國的地圖」裡？台北路名藏著滿滿的台灣過往 |url=https://www.gvm.com.tw/article/71003 |website=遠見 |publisher=天下文化 |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw |date=February 21, 2020}}</ref>
The shrine was elevated in Rank to Grand Shrine (''jingū'') in 1944 ([[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]] 19) when [[Amaterasu]] was enshrined, making it the highest-Ranking shrine in Taiwan.<ref name="hsu"/> The opening ceremony of the new shrine was planned to be on October 28. However, on October 23, 1944, a cargo plane lost control and crashed atop the mountain where the Taiwan Grand Shrine was located, heavily damaging roughly half of the shrine.<ref>{{cite web |author1=郭俊麟 |title=淺談臺灣今昔地圖與歷史航照在中學地理資訊教育的意義 |url=http://epaper.ntu.edu.tw/view.php?id=10773 |website=GIS 教育推廣 電子報 |publisher=[[National Taiwan University]] |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw}}</ref> The shrine was never fully repaired due to [[:en:Surrender of Japan|Japan's surrender after World War II]], and much of the shrine's materials were taken for construction projects elsewhere. In its place, the [[:en:Grand Hotel (Taipei City)|Grand Hotel]] was constructed in 1952, where it remains as a prominent landmark of the city.<ref name="hsu"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Taiwan Grand Shrine.jpg|A [[:en:Postcard|Postcard]] issued by the [[:en:Governor-General of Taiwan|Governor-General of Taiwan]] of Taiwan Grand Shrine. The [[:en:Imperial Seal of Japan|Imperial Seal]] is visible in the upper-right section. File:Taiwan_(Japanese_Colony)_1932_bank_note_-_10_yen_(front).jpg|Taiwan banknotes (10 yen banknotes) during the Japanese colonial era with the Taiwan Grand Shrine printed File:1923rd year 日本皇太子裕仁訪拜臺灣神社_Japanese_Crown_Prince_Hirohito_on_the_way_to_Taiwan_Grand_Shrine.jpg|Prince Hirohito visits the Taiwan Grand Shrine during the Crown Prince Hirohito's visit to Taiwan (April 17, 1923) File:Copper cattle of Taiwan Jinja.JPG|This copper bull was originally located at Taiwan Grand Shrine but was moved to in front of [[National Taiwan Museum]] after the end of World War II. </gallery>
==See also== *[[:en:Shintoism in Taiwan|Shintoism in Taiwan]] *[[:en:List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan|List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:ضريح تايوان الكبير]][[commons:Category:Taiwan Grand Shrine]][[en:Taiwan Grand Shrine]][[es:Gran Santuario de Taiwán]][[fa:معبد بزرگ تایوان]][[fr:Taiwan-jingū]][[it:Grande Santuario di Taiwan]][[ja:台湾神宮]][[ko:타이완 신궁]][[zh_min_nan:Tâi-oân Sîn-kiong]][[zh:臺灣神宮]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:ضريح تايوان الكبير]][[commons:Category:Taiwan Grand Shrine]][[en:Taiwan Grand Shrine]][[es:Gran Santuario de Taiwán]][[fa:معبد بزرگ تایوان]][[fr:Taiwan-jingū]][[it:Grande Santuario di Taiwan]][[ja:台湾神宮]][[ko:타이완 신궁]][[zh_min_nan:Tâi-oân Sîn-kiong]][[zh:臺灣神宮]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:ضريح تايوان الكبير]][[sn:commons:Category:Taiwan Grand Shrine]][[en:Taiwan Grand Shrine]][[es:Gran Santuario de Taiwán]][[fa:معبد بزرگ تایوان]][[fr:Taiwan-jingū]][[it:Grande Santuario di Taiwan]][[ja:台湾神宮]][[ko:타이완 신궁]][[zh-min-nan:Tâi-oân Sîn-kiong]][[zh:臺灣神宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q708921]] Wikidata name: Taiwan Grand Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:42, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Former Shinto shrine in Taipei, Taiwan}} [[File:Painting of Taiwan Grand Shrine.jpg|thumb|300px|A painting of Taiwan Grand Shrine during [[:en:Taiwan under Japanese rule|Japanese colonial rule]]. At the bottom of the painting is the Keelung River and in the lower-right corner the Meiji Bridge is partially visible.]]
The '''Taiwan Grand Shrine''' ({{CJKV|c=臺灣神宮|r=Taiwan Jingū|p=Táiwān Shéngōng}}) was the highest Ranking Japanese [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in Taiwan during [[:en:Taiwan under Japanese rule|Japanese colonial rule]]. It was located in [[:en:Taipei|Taihoku]], [[:en:Taiwan|Taiwan]] (now [[:en:Zhongshan District, Taipei|Zhongshan District, Taipei]]). Among the officially sanctioned Shinto shrines in Taiwan, Taiwan Grand Shrine held the highest Rank of them all. The [[:en:Grand Hotel (Taipei City)|Grand Hotel]] stands at the shrine's former site.
== History == [[File:Taiwan-Grand-Shrine-Jingu-Map-1920s.png|thumb|300px|Taiwan Grand Shrine Map (1920s) records the location of each object and detailed information.]] Following the death of [[:en:Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|Prince Yoshihisa]] in 1895, the [[:en:Governor-General of Taiwan|Governor-General of Taiwan]] [[Nogi Maresuke]] began planning for a shrine in Yoshihisa's honor. Originally, the plan was to construct the shrine at Yuanshan Park ({{lang|zh-Hant|圓山公園}}, now part of [[:en:Taipei Expo Park|Taipei Expo Park]]); however, Nogi's successor [[:en:Kodama Gentarō|Kodama Gentarō]] and chief planner [[:en:Gotō Shinpei|Gotō Shinpei]] decided to move it across the [[:en:Keelung River|Keelung River]] to Jiantan Mountain ({{lang|zh-Hant|劍潭山}}) for the site's higher elevation. The vantage point would allow the shrine to overlook the entire city, making it symbolic for the Japanese Empire's colonial power.<ref name="lee">{{cite book |author=李東明 |title=永遠的北淡線 |publisher=玉山社 |isbn=957-8246-32-3 |pages=88–90 |language=zh-tw |chapter=宮之下 - 台灣神社下的火車站|year=2000 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |title=第四章 日治時期臺灣各級神社的選址與設域的空間特性 |pages=137–140 |url=http://rportal.lib.ntnu.edu.tw/bitstream/20.500.12235/94400/4/n088723001004.pdf |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |publisher=[[National Taiwan Normal University]] |language=zh-tw}}</ref> The construction lasted between 1900 and 1901, and the completed shrine was dedicated to Yoshihisa and the {{nihongo|Kaitaku Sanjin|開拓三神|Three Kami Deities of Pioneering}}.<ref name="hsu">{{cite web |author1=許書維 |title=臺灣神社誌 |url=https://www.th.gov.tw/epaper/site/page/78/1078 |website=國史館臺灣文獻館 |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw}}</ref> In 1915, a railway station named {{nihongo|Miyanoshita Station|宮ノ下乘降場||now [[:en:Jiantan metro station|Jiantan metro station]]}} was placed at the foot of Jiantan Mountain to serve the shrine.<ref name="lee"/> On April 12, 1923, [[:en:Crown Prince|Crown Prince]] [[:en:Hirohito|Hirohito]], who would become Emperor Shōwa three years later, embarked on a two-week tour of Taiwan. In preparation for his visit to the shrine, {{nihongo|Chokushi Road|勅使街道|Chokushi kaidō|present-day Chungshan North Road}} was created leading up to the shrine from the city, with the {{nihongo|Meiji Bridge|明治橋}} crossing the Keelung River.<ref>{{cite web |author1=一流人 |title=我們住在「中國的地圖」裡？台北路名藏著滿滿的台灣過往 |url=https://www.gvm.com.tw/article/71003 |website=遠見 |publisher=天下文化 |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw |date=February 21, 2020}}</ref>
The shrine was elevated in Rank to Grand Shrine (''jingū'') in 1944 ([[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]] 19) when [[Amaterasu]] was enshrined, making it the highest-Ranking shrine in Taiwan.<ref name="hsu"/> The opening ceremony of the new shrine was planned to be on October 28. However, on October 23, 1944, a cargo plane lost control and crashed atop the mountain where the Taiwan Grand Shrine was located, heavily damaging roughly half of the shrine.<ref>{{cite web |author1=郭俊麟 |title=淺談臺灣今昔地圖與歷史航照在中學地理資訊教育的意義 |url=http://epaper.ntu.edu.tw/view.php?id=10773 |website=GIS 教育推廣 電子報 |publisher=[[National Taiwan University]] |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw}}</ref> The shrine was never fully repaired due to [[:en:Surrender of Japan|Japan's surrender after World War II]], and much of the shrine's materials were taken for construction projects elsewhere. In its place, the [[:en:Grand Hotel (Taipei City)|Grand Hotel]] was constructed in 1952, where it remains as a prominent landmark of the city.<ref name="hsu"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Taiwan Grand Shrine.jpg|A [[:en:Postcard|Postcard]] issued by the [[:en:Governor-General of Taiwan|Governor-General of Taiwan]] of Taiwan Grand Shrine. The [[:en:Imperial Seal of Japan|Imperial Seal]] is visible in the upper-right section. File:Taiwan_(Japanese_Colony)_1932_bank_note_-_10_yen_(front).jpg|Taiwan banknotes (10 yen banknotes) during the Japanese colonial era with the Taiwan Grand Shrine printed File:1923rd year 日本皇太子裕仁訪拜臺灣神社_Japanese_Crown_Prince_Hirohito_on_the_way_to_Taiwan_Grand_Shrine.jpg|Prince Hirohito visits the Taiwan Grand Shrine during the Crown Prince Hirohito's visit to Taiwan (April 17, 1923) File:Copper cattle of Taiwan Jinja.JPG|This copper bull was originally located at Taiwan Grand Shrine but was moved to in front of [[National Taiwan Museum]] after the end of World War II. </gallery>
==See also== *[[:en:Shintoism in Taiwan|Shintoism in Taiwan]] *[[:en:List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan|List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:ضريح تايوان الكبير]][[co:commons:Category:Taiwan Grand Shrine]][[en:Taiwan Grand Shrine]][[es:Gran Santuario de Taiwán]][[fa:معبد بزرگ تایوان]][[fr:Taiwan-jingū]][[it:Grande Santuario di Taiwan]][[ja:台湾神宮]][[ko:타이완 신궁]][[zh-min-nan:Tâi-oân Sîn-kiong]][[zh:臺灣神宮]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:ضريح تايوان الكبير]][[co:commons:Category:Taiwan Grand Shrine]][[en:Taiwan Grand Shrine]][[es:Gran Santuario de Taiwán]][[fa:معبد بزرگ تایوان]][[fr:Taiwan-jingū]][[it:Grande Santuario di Taiwan]][[ja:台湾神宮]][[ko:타이완 신궁]][[zh-min-nan:Tâi-oân Sîn-kiong]][[zh:臺灣神宮]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:ضريح تايوان الكبير]][[sn:commons:Category:Taiwan Grand Shrine]][[en:Taiwan Grand Shrine]][[es:Gran Santuario de Taiwán]][[fa:معبد بزرگ تایوان]][[fr:Taiwan-jingū]][[it:Grande Santuario di Taiwan]][[ja:台湾神宮]][[ko:타이완 신궁]][[zh-min-nan:Tâi-oân Sîn-kiong]][[zh:臺灣神宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q708921]] Wikidata name: Taiwan Grand Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:42, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Former Shinto shrine in Taipei, Taiwan}} [[File:Painting of Taiwan Grand Shrine.jpg|thumb|300px|A painting of Taiwan Grand Shrine during [[:en:Taiwan under Japanese rule|Japanese colonial rule]]. At the bottom of the painting is the Keelung River and in the lower-right corner the Meiji Bridge is partially visible.]]
The '''Taiwan Grand Shrine''' ({{CJKV|c=臺灣神宮|r=Taiwan Jingū|p=Táiwān Shéngōng}}) was the highest Ranking Japanese [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in Taiwan during [[:en:Taiwan under Japanese rule|Japanese colonial rule]]. It was located in [[:en:Taipei|Taihoku]], [[:en:Taiwan|Taiwan]] (now [[:en:Zhongshan District, Taipei|Zhongshan District, Taipei]]). Among the officially sanctioned Shinto shrines in Taiwan, Taiwan Grand Shrine held the highest Rank of them all. The [[:en:Grand Hotel (Taipei City)|Grand Hotel]] stands at the shrine's former site.
== History == [[File:Taiwan-Grand-Shrine-Jingu-Map-1920s.png|thumb|300px|Taiwan Grand Shrine Map (1920s) records the location of each object and detailed information.]] Following the death of [[:en:Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|Prince Yoshihisa]] in 1895, the [[:en:Governor-General of Taiwan|Governor-General of Taiwan]] [[Nogi Maresuke]] began planning for a shrine in Yoshihisa's honor. Originally, the plan was to construct the shrine at Yuanshan Park ({{lang|zh-Hant|圓山公園}}, now part of [[:en:Taipei Expo Park|Taipei Expo Park]]); however, Nogi's successor [[:en:Kodama Gentarō|Kodama Gentarō]] and chief planner [[:en:Gotō Shinpei|Gotō Shinpei]] decided to move it across the [[:en:Keelung River|Keelung River]] to Jiantan Mountain ({{lang|zh-Hant|劍潭山}}) for the site's higher elevation. The vantage point would allow the shrine to overlook the entire city, making it symbolic for the Japanese Empire's colonial power.<ref name="lee">{{cite book |author=李東明 |title=永遠的北淡線 |publisher=玉山社 |isbn=957-8246-32-3 |pages=88–90 |language=zh-tw |chapter=宮之下 - 台灣神社下的火車站|year=2000 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |title=第四章 日治時期臺灣各級神社的選址與設域的空間特性 |pages=137–140 |url=http://rportal.lib.ntnu.edu.tw/bitstream/20.500.12235/94400/4/n088723001004.pdf |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |publisher=[[National Taiwan Normal University]] |language=zh-tw}}</ref> The construction lasted between 1900 and 1901, and the completed shrine was dedicated to Yoshihisa and the {{nihongo|Kaitaku Sanjin|開拓三神|Three Kami Deities of Pioneering}}.<ref name="hsu">{{cite web |author1=許書維 |title=臺灣神社誌 |url=https://www.th.gov.tw/epaper/site/page/78/1078 |website=國史館臺灣文獻館 |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw}}</ref> In 1915, a railway station named {{nihongo|Miyanoshita Station|宮ノ下乘降場||now [[:en:Jiantan metro station|Jiantan metro station]]}} was placed at the foot of Jiantan Mountain to serve the shrine.<ref name="lee"/> On April 12, 1923, [[:en:Crown Prince|Crown Prince]] [[:en:Hirohito|Hirohito]], who would become Emperor Shōwa three years later, embarked on a two-week tour of Taiwan. In preparation for his visit to the shrine, {{nihongo|Chokushi Road|勅使街道|Chokushi kaidō|present-day Chungshan North Road}} was created leading up to the shrine from the city, with the {{nihongo|Meiji Bridge|明治橋}} crossing the Keelung River.<ref>{{cite web |author1=一流人 |title=我們住在「中國的地圖」裡？台北路名藏著滿滿的台灣過往 |url=https://www.gvm.com.tw/article/71003 |website=遠見 |publisher=天下文化 |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw |date=February 21, 2020}}</ref>
The shrine was elevated in Rank to Grand Shrine (''jingū'') in 1944 ([[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]] 19) when [[Amaterasu]] was enshrined, making it the highest-Ranking shrine in Taiwan.<ref name="hsu"/> The opening ceremony of the new shrine was planned to be on October 28. However, on October 23, 1944, a cargo plane lost control and crashed atop the mountain where the Taiwan Grand Shrine was located, heavily damaging roughly half of the shrine.<ref>{{cite web |author1=郭俊麟 |title=淺談臺灣今昔地圖與歷史航照在中學地理資訊教育的意義 |url=http://epaper.ntu.edu.tw/view.php?id=10773 |website=GIS 教育推廣 電子報 |publisher=[[National Taiwan University]] |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw}}</ref> The shrine was never fully repaired due to [[:en:Surrender of Japan|Japan's surrender after World War II]], and much of the shrine's materials were taken for construction projects elsewhere. In its place, the [[:en:Grand Hotel (Taipei City)|Grand Hotel]] was constructed in 1952, where it remains as a prominent landmark of the city.<ref name="hsu"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Taiwan Grand Shrine.jpg|A [[:en:Postcard|Postcard]] issued by the [[:en:Governor-General of Taiwan|Governor-General of Taiwan]] of Taiwan Grand Shrine. The [[:en:Imperial Seal of Japan|Imperial Seal]] is visible in the upper-right section. File:Taiwan_(Japanese_Colony)_1932_bank_note_-_10_yen_(front).jpg|Taiwan banknotes (10 yen banknotes) during the Japanese colonial era with the Taiwan Grand Shrine printed File:1923rd year 日本皇太子裕仁訪拜臺灣神社_Japanese_Crown_Prince_Hirohito_on_the_way_to_Taiwan_Grand_Shrine.jpg|Prince Hirohito visits the Taiwan Grand Shrine during the Crown Prince Hirohito's visit to Taiwan (April 17, 1923) File:Copper cattle of Taiwan Jinja.JPG|This copper bull was originally located at Taiwan Grand Shrine but was moved to in front of [[National Taiwan Museum]] after the end of World War II. </gallery>
==See also== *[[:en:Shintoism in Taiwan|Shintoism in Taiwan]] *[[:en:List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan|List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan]]

The vantage point would allow the shrine to overlook the entire city, making it symbolic for the Japanese Empire's colonial power.<ref name="lee">{{cite book |author=李東明 |title=永遠的北淡線 |publisher=玉山社 |isbn=957-8246-32-3 |pages=88–90 |language=zh-tw |chapter=宮之下 - 台灣神社下的火車站|year=2000 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |title=第四章 日治時期臺灣各級神社的選址與設域的空間特性 |pages=137–140 |url=http://rportal.lib.ntnu.edu.tw/bitstream/20.500.12235/94400/4/n088723001004.pdf |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |publisher=[[National Taiwan Normal University]] |language=zh-tw}}</ref> The construction lasted between 1900 and 1901, and the completed shrine was dedicated to Yoshihisa and the {{nihongo|Kaitaku Sanjin|開拓三神|Three Kami Deities of Pioneering}}.<ref name="hsu">{{cite web |author1=許書維 |title=臺灣神社誌 |url=https://www.th.gov.tw/epaper/site/page/78/1078 |website=國史館臺灣文獻館 |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw}}</ref> In 1915, a railway station named {{nihongo|Miyanoshita Station|宮ノ下乘降場||now {{ill|Jiantan metro station|en|Jiantan metro station|WD=Q698433|lt=Jiantan metro station}}}} was placed at the foot of Jiantan Mountain to serve the shrine.<ref name="lee"/> On April 12, 1923, [[:en:Crown Prince|Crown Prince]] {{ill|Hirohito|en|Hirohito|WD=Q34479|lt=Hirohito}}, who would become Emperor Shōwa three years later, embarked on a two-week tour of Taiwan. In preparation for his visit to the shrine, {{nihongo|Chokushi Road|勅使街道|Chokushi kaidō|present-day Chungshan North Road}} was created leading up to the shrine from the city, with the {{nihongo|Meiji Bridge|明治橋}} crossing the Keelung River.<ref>{{cite web |author1=一流人 |title=我們住在「中國的地圖」裡？台北路名藏著滿滿的台灣過往 |url=https://www.gvm.com.tw/article/71003 |website=遠見 |publisher=天下文化 |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw |date=February 21, 2020}}</ref>
The shrine was elevated in Rank to Grand Shrine (''jingū'') in 1944 ({{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa}} 19) when [[Amaterasu]] was enshrined, making it the highest-Ranking shrine in Taiwan.<ref name="hsu"/> The opening ceremony of the new shrine was planned to be on October 28. However, on October 23, 1944, a cargo plane lost control and crashed atop the mountain where the Taiwan Grand Shrine was located, heavily damaging roughly half of the shrine.<ref>{{cite web |author1=郭俊麟 |title=淺談臺灣今昔地圖與歷史航照在中學地理資訊教育的意義 |url=http://epaper.ntu.edu.tw/view.php?id=10773 |website=GIS 教育推廣 電子報 |publisher=[[National Taiwan University]] |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw}}</ref> The shrine was never fully repaired due to {{ill|Surrender of Japan|en|Surrender of Japan|WD=Q6540361|lt=Japan's surrender after World War II}}, and much of the shrine's materials were taken for construction projects elsewhere. In its place, the [[:en:Grand Hotel (Taipei City)|Grand Hotel]] was constructed in 1952, where it remains as a prominent landmark of the city.<ref name="hsu"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Taiwan Grand Shrine.jpg|A {{ill|Postcard|en|Postcard|WD=Q192425|lt=Postcard}} issued by the {{ill|Governor-General of Taiwan|en|Governor-General of Taiwan|WD=Q701172|lt=Governor-General of Taiwan}} of Taiwan Grand Shrine. The [[:en:Imperial Seal of Japan|Imperial Seal]] is visible in the upper-right section. File:Taiwan_(Japanese_Colony)_1932_bank_note_-_10_yen_(front).jpg|Taiwan banknotes (10 yen banknotes) during the Japanese colonial era with the Taiwan Grand Shrine printed File:1923rd year 日本皇太子裕仁訪拜臺灣神社_Japanese_Crown_Prince_Hirohito_on_the_way_to_Taiwan_Grand_Shrine.jpg|Prince Hirohito visits the Taiwan Grand Shrine during the Crown Prince Hirohito's visit to Taiwan (April 17, 1923) File:Copper cattle of Taiwan Jinja.JPG|This copper bull was originally located at Taiwan Grand Shrine but was moved to in front of [[National Taiwan Museum]] after the end of World War II. </gallery>
==See also== *[[:en:Shintoism in Taiwan|Shintoism in Taiwan]] *{{ill|List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan|en|List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan|WD=Q6596888|lt=List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:ضريح تايوان الكبير]][[co:commons:Category:Taiwan Grand Shrine]][[en:Taiwan Grand Shrine]][[es:Gran Santuario de Taiwán]][[fa:معبد بزرگ تایوان]][[fr:Taiwan-jingū]][[it:Grande Santuario di Taiwan]][[ja:台湾神宮]][[ko:타이완 신궁]][[zh-min-nan:Tâi-oân Sîn-kiong]][[zh:臺灣神宮]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:ضريح تايوان الكبير]][[co:commons:Category:Taiwan Grand Shrine]][[en:Taiwan Grand Shrine]][[es:Gran Santuario de Taiwán]][[fa:معبد بزرگ تایوان]][[fr:Taiwan-jingū]][[it:Grande Santuario di Taiwan]][[ja:台湾神宮]][[ko:타이완 신궁]][[zh-min-nan:Tâi-oân Sîn-kiong]][[zh:臺灣神宮]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:ضريح تايوان الكبير]][[sn:commons:Category:Taiwan Grand Shrine]][[en:Taiwan Grand Shrine]][[es:Gran Santuario de Taiwán]][[fa:معبد بزرگ تایوان]][[fr:Taiwan-jingū]][[it:Grande Santuario di Taiwan]][[ja:台湾神宮]][[ko:타이완 신궁]][[zh-min-nan:Tâi-oân Sîn-kiong]][[zh:臺灣神宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q708921]] Wikidata name: Taiwan Grand Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:42, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Former Shinto shrine in Taipei, Taiwan}} [[File:Painting of Taiwan Grand Shrine.jpg|thumb|300px|A painting of Taiwan Grand Shrine during {{ill|Taiwan under Japanese rule|en|Taiwan under Japanese rule|WD=Q137816|lt=Japanese colonial rule}}. At the bottom of the painting is the Keelung River and in the lower-right corner the Meiji Bridge is partially visible.]]
The '''Taiwan Grand Shrine''' ({{CJKV|c=臺灣神宮|r=Taiwan Jingū|p=Táiwān Shéngōng}}) was the highest Ranking Japanese {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} in Taiwan during {{ill|Taiwan under Japanese rule|en|Taiwan under Japanese rule|WD=Q137816|lt=Japanese colonial rule}}. It was located in {{ill|Taipei|en|Taipei|WD=Q1867|lt=Taihoku}}, {{ill|Taiwan|en|Taiwan|WD=Q865|lt=Taiwan}} (now {{ill|Zhongshan District, Taipei|en|Zhongshan District, Taipei|WD=Q271167|lt=Zhongshan District, Taipei}}). Among the officially sanctioned Shinto shrines in Taiwan, Taiwan Grand Shrine held the highest Rank of them all. The [[:en:Grand Hotel (Taipei City)|Grand Hotel]] stands at the shrine's former site.
== History == [[File:Taiwan-Grand-Shrine-Jingu-Map-1920s.png|thumb|300px|Taiwan Grand Shrine Map (1920s) records the location of each object and detailed information.]] Following the death of {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|en|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|WD=Q3197569|lt=Prince Yoshihisa}} in 1895, the {{ill|Governor-General of Taiwan|en|Governor-General of Taiwan|WD=Q701172|lt=Governor-General of Taiwan}} [[Nogi Maresuke]] began planning for a shrine in Yoshihisa's honor. Originally, the plan was to construct the shrine at Yuanshan Park ({{lang|zh-Hant|圓山公園}}, now part of {{ill|Taipei Expo Park|en|Taipei Expo Park|WD=Q14197588|lt=Taipei Expo Park}}); however, Nogi's successor {{ill|Kodama Gentarō|en|Kodama Gentarō|WD=Q72515|lt=Kodama Gentarō}} and chief planner {{ill|Gotō Shinpei|en|Gotō Shinpei|WD=Q562492|lt=Gotō Shinpei}} decided to move it across the {{ill|Keelung River|en|Keelung River|WD=Q708976|lt=Keelung River}} to Jiantan Mountain ({{lang|zh-Hant|劍潭山}}) for the site's higher elevation. The vantage point would allow the shrine to overlook the entire city, making it symbolic for the Japanese Empire's colonial power.<ref name="lee">{{cite book |author=李東明 |title=永遠的北淡線 |publisher=玉山社 |isbn=957-8246-32-3 |pages=88–90 |language=zh-tw |chapter=宮之下 - 台灣神社下的火車站|year=2000 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |title=第四章 日治時期臺灣各級神社的選址與設域的空間特性 |pages=137–140 |url=http://rportal.lib.ntnu.edu.tw/bitstream/20.500.12235/94400/4/n088723001004.pdf |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |publisher=[[National Taiwan Normal University]] |language=zh-tw}}</ref> The construction lasted between 1900 and 1901, and the completed shrine was dedicated to Yoshihisa and the {{nihongo|Kaitaku Sanjin|開拓三神|Three Kami Deities of Pioneering}}.<ref name="hsu">{{cite web |author1=許書維 |title=臺灣神社誌 |url=https://www.th.gov.tw/epaper/site/page/78/1078 |website=國史館臺灣文獻館 |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw}}</ref> In 1915, a railway station named {{nihongo|Miyanoshita Station|宮ノ下乘降場||now {{ill|Jiantan metro station|en|Jiantan metro station|WD=Q698433|lt=Jiantan metro station}}}} was placed at the foot of Jiantan Mountain to serve the shrine.<ref name="lee"/> On April 12, 1923, [[:en:Crown Prince|Crown Prince]] {{ill|Hirohito|en|Hirohito|WD=Q34479|lt=Hirohito}}, who would become Emperor Shōwa three years later, embarked on a two-week tour of Taiwan. In preparation for his visit to the shrine, {{nihongo|Chokushi Road|勅使街道|Chokushi kaidō|present-day Chungshan North Road}} was created leading up to the shrine from the city, with the {{nihongo|Meiji Bridge|明治橋}} crossing the Keelung River.<ref>{{cite web |author1=一流人 |title=我們住在「中國的地圖」裡？台北路名藏著滿滿的台灣過往 |url=https://www.gvm.com.tw/article/71003 |website=遠見 |publisher=天下文化 |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw |date=February 21, 2020}}</ref>
The shrine was elevated in Rank to Grand Shrine (''jingū'') in 1944 ({{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa}} 19) when [[Amaterasu]] was enshrined, making it the highest-Ranking shrine in Taiwan.<ref name="hsu"/> The opening ceremony of the new shrine was planned to be on October 28. However, on October 23, 1944, a cargo plane lost control and crashed atop the mountain where the Taiwan Grand Shrine was located, heavily damaging roughly half of the shrine.<ref>{{cite web |author1=郭俊麟 |title=淺談臺灣今昔地圖與歷史航照在中學地理資訊教育的意義 |url=http://epaper.ntu.edu.tw/view.php?id=10773 |website=GIS 教育推廣 電子報 |publisher=[[National Taiwan University]] |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw}}</ref> The shrine was never fully repaired due to {{ill|Surrender of Japan|en|Surrender of Japan|WD=Q6540361|lt=Japan's surrender after World War II}}, and much of the shrine's materials were taken for construction projects elsewhere. In its place, the [[:en:Grand Hotel (Taipei City)|Grand Hotel]] was constructed in 1952, where it remains as a prominent landmark of the city.<ref name="hsu"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Taiwan Grand Shrine.jpg|A {{ill|Postcard|en|Postcard|WD=Q192425|lt=Postcard}} issued by the {{ill|Governor-General of Taiwan|en|Governor-General of Taiwan|WD=Q701172|lt=Governor-General of Taiwan}} of Taiwan Grand Shrine. The [[:en:Imperial Seal of Japan|Imperial Seal]] is visible in the upper-right section. File:Taiwan_(Japanese_Colony)_1932_bank_note_-_10_yen_(front).jpg|Taiwan banknotes (10 yen banknotes) during the Japanese colonial era with the Taiwan Grand Shrine printed File:1923rd year 日本皇太子裕仁訪拜臺灣神社_Japanese_Crown_Prince_Hirohito_on_the_way_to_Taiwan_Grand_Shrine.jpg|Prince Hirohito visits the Taiwan Grand Shrine during the Crown Prince Hirohito's visit to Taiwan (April 17, 1923) File:Copper cattle of Taiwan Jinja.JPG|This copper bull was originally located at Taiwan Grand Shrine but was moved to in front of [[National Taiwan Museum]] after the end of World War II. </gallery>
==See also== *[[:en:Shintoism in Taiwan|Shintoism in Taiwan]] *{{ill|List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan|en|List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan|WD=Q6596888|lt=List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:ضريح تايوان الكبير]][[co:commons:Category:Taiwan Grand Shrine]][[en:Taiwan Grand Shrine]][[es:Gran Santuario de Taiwán]][[fa:معبد بزرگ تایوان]][[fr:Taiwan-jingū]][[it:Grande Santuario di Taiwan]][[ja:台湾神宮]][[ko:타이완 신궁]][[zh-min-nan:Tâi-oân Sîn-kiong]][[zh:臺灣神宮]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:ضريح تايوان الكبير]][[co:commons:Category:Taiwan Grand Shrine]][[en:Taiwan Grand Shrine]][[es:Gran Santuario de Taiwán]][[fa:معبد بزرگ تایوان]][[fr:Taiwan-jingū]][[it:Grande Santuario di Taiwan]][[ja:台湾神宮]][[ko:타이완 신궁]][[zh-min-nan:Tâi-oân Sîn-kiong]][[zh:臺灣神宮]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:ضريح تايوان الكبير]][[sn:commons:Category:Taiwan Grand Shrine]][[en:Taiwan Grand Shrine]][[es:Gran Santuario de Taiwán]][[fa:معبد بزرگ تایوان]][[fr:Taiwan-jingū]][[it:Grande Santuario di Taiwan]][[ja:台湾神宮]][[ko:타이완 신궁]][[zh-min-nan:Tâi-oân Sîn-kiong]][[zh:臺灣神宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q708921]] Wikidata name: Taiwan Grand Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:42, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Former Shinto shrine in Taipei, Taiwan}} [[File:Painting of Taiwan Grand Shrine.jpg|thumb|300px|A painting of Taiwan Grand Shrine during {{ill|Taiwan under Japanese rule|en|Taiwan under Japanese rule|WD=Q137816|lt=Japanese colonial rule}}. At the bottom of the painting is the Keelung River and in the lower-right corner the Meiji Bridge is partially visible.]]
The '''Taiwan Grand Shrine''' ({{CJKV|c=臺灣神宮|r=Taiwan Jingū|p=Táiwān Shéngōng}}) was the highest Ranking Japanese {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} in Taiwan during {{ill|Taiwan under Japanese rule|en|Taiwan under Japanese rule|WD=Q137816|lt=Japanese colonial rule}}. It was located in {{ill|Taipei|en|Taipei|WD=Q1867|lt=Taihoku}}, {{ill|Taiwan|en|Taiwan|WD=Q865|lt=Taiwan}} (now {{ill|Zhongshan District, Taipei|en|Zhongshan District, Taipei|WD=Q271167|lt=Zhongshan District, Taipei}}). Among the officially sanctioned Shinto shrines in Taiwan, Taiwan Grand Shrine held the highest Rank of them all. The [[:en:Grand Hotel (Taipei City)|Grand Hotel]] stands at the shrine's former site.
== History == [[File:Taiwan-Grand-Shrine-Jingu-Map-1920s.png|thumb|300px|Taiwan Grand Shrine Map (1920s) records the location of each object and detailed information.]] Following the death of {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|en|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|WD=Q3197569|lt=Prince Yoshihisa}} in 1895, the {{ill|Governor-General of Taiwan|en|Governor-General of Taiwan|WD=Q701172|lt=Governor-General of Taiwan}} [[Nogi Maresuke]] began planning for a shrine in Yoshihisa's honor. Originally, the plan was to construct the shrine at Yuanshan Park ({{lang|zh-Hant|圓山公園}}, now part of {{ill|Taipei Expo Park|en|Taipei Expo Park|WD=Q14197588|lt=Taipei Expo Park}}); however, Nogi's successor {{ill|Kodama Gentarō|en|Kodama Gentarō|WD=Q72515|lt=Kodama Gentarō}} and chief planner {{ill|Gotō Shinpei|en|Gotō Shinpei|WD=Q562492|lt=Gotō Shinpei}} decided to move it across the {{ill|Keelung River|en|Keelung River|WD=Q708976|lt=Keelung River}} to Jiantan Mountain ({{lang|zh-Hant|劍潭山}}) for the site's higher elevation. The vantage point would allow the shrine to overlook the entire city, making it symbolic for the Japanese Empire's colonial power.<ref name="lee">{{cite book |author=李東明 |title=永遠的北淡線 |publisher=玉山社 |isbn=957-8246-32-3 |pages=88–90 |language=zh-tw |chapter=宮之下 - 台灣神社下的火車站|year=2000 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |title=第四章 日治時期臺灣各級神社的選址與設域的空間特性 |pages=137–140 |url=http://rportal.lib.ntnu.edu.tw/bitstream/20.500.12235/94400/4/n088723001004.pdf |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |publisher=[[National Taiwan Normal University]] |language=zh-tw}}</ref> The construction lasted between 1900 and 1901, and the completed shrine was dedicated to Yoshihisa and the {{nihongo|Kaitaku Sanjin|開拓三神|Three Kami Deities of Pioneering}}.<ref name="hsu">{{cite web |author1=許書維 |title=臺灣神社誌 |url=https://www.th.gov.tw/epaper/site/page/78/1078 |website=國史館臺灣文獻館 |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw}}</ref> In 1915, a railway station named {{nihongo|Miyanoshita Station|宮ノ下乘降場||now {{ill|Jiantan metro station|en|Jiantan metro station|WD=Q698433|lt=Jiantan metro station}}}} was placed at the foot of Jiantan Mountain to serve the shrine.<ref name="lee"/> On April 12, 1923, [[:en:Crown Prince|Crown Prince]] {{ill|Hirohito|en|Hirohito|WD=Q34479|lt=Hirohito}}, who would become Emperor Shōwa three years later, embarked on a two-week tour of Taiwan. In preparation for his visit to the shrine, {{nihongo|Chokushi Road|勅使街道|Chokushi kaidō|present-day Chungshan North Road}} was created leading up to the shrine from the city, with the {{nihongo|Meiji Bridge|明治橋}} crossing the Keelung River.<ref>{{cite web |author1=一流人 |title=我們住在「中國的地圖」裡？台北路名藏著滿滿的台灣過往 |url=https://www.gvm.com.tw/article/71003 |website=遠見 |publisher=天下文化 |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw |date=February 21, 2020}}</ref>
The shrine was elevated in Rank to Grand Shrine (''jingū'') in 1944 ({{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa}} 19) when [[Amaterasu]] was enshrined, making it the highest-Ranking shrine in Taiwan.<ref name="hsu"/> The opening ceremony of the new shrine was planned to be on October 28. However, on October 23, 1944, a cargo plane lost control and crashed atop the mountain where the Taiwan Grand Shrine was located, heavily damaging roughly half of the shrine.<ref>{{cite web |author1=郭俊麟 |title=淺談臺灣今昔地圖與歷史航照在中學地理資訊教育的意義 |url=http://epaper.ntu.edu.tw/view.php?id=10773 |website=GIS 教育推廣 電子報 |publisher=[[National Taiwan University]] |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw}}</ref> The shrine was never fully repaired due to {{ill|Surrender of Japan|en|Surrender of Japan|WD=Q6540361|lt=Japan's surrender after World War II}}, and much of the shrine's materials were taken for construction projects elsewhere. In its place, the [[:en:Grand Hotel (Taipei City)|Grand Hotel]] was constructed in 1952, where it remains as a prominent landmark of the city.<ref name="hsu"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Taiwan Grand Shrine.jpg|A {{ill|Postcard|en|Postcard|WD=Q192425|lt=Postcard}} issued by the {{ill|Governor-General of Taiwan|en|Governor-General of Taiwan|WD=Q701172|lt=Governor-General of Taiwan}} of Taiwan Grand Shrine. The [[:en:Imperial Seal of Japan|Imperial Seal]] is visible in the upper-right section. File:Taiwan_(Japanese_Colony)_1932_bank_note_-_10_yen_(front).jpg|Taiwan banknotes (10 yen banknotes) during the Japanese colonial era with the Taiwan Grand Shrine printed File:1923rd year 日本皇太子裕仁訪拜臺灣神社_Japanese_Crown_Prince_Hirohito_on_the_way_to_Taiwan_Grand_Shrine.jpg|Prince Hirohito visits the Taiwan Grand Shrine during the Crown Prince Hirohito's visit to Taiwan (April 17, 1923) File:Copper cattle of Taiwan Jinja.JPG|This copper bull was originally located at Taiwan Grand Shrine but was moved to in front of [[National Taiwan Museum]] after the end of World War II. </gallery>
==See also== *[[:en:Shintoism in Taiwan|Shintoism in Taiwan]] *{{ill|List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan|en|List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan|WD=Q6596888|lt=List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan}}

Originally, the plan was to construct the shrine at Yuanshan Park ({{lang|zh-Hant|圓山公園}}, now part of {{ill|Taipei Expo Park|en|Taipei Expo Park|WD=Q14197588|lt=Taipei Expo Park}}); however, Nogi's successor {{ill|Kodama Gentarō|en|Kodama Gentarō|WD=Q72515|lt=Kodama Gentarō}} and chief planner {{ill|Gotō Shinpei|en|Gotō Shinpei|WD=Q562492|lt=Gotō Shinpei}} decided to move it across the {{ill|Keelung River|en|Keelung River|WD=Q708976|lt=Keelung River}} to Jiantan Mountain ({{lang|zh-Hant|劍潭山}}) for the site's higher elevation. The vantage point would allow the shrine to overlook the entire city, making it symbolic for the Japanese Empire's colonial power.<ref name="lee">{{cite book |author=李東明 |title=永遠的北淡線 |publisher=玉山社 |isbn=957-8246-32-3 |pages=88–90 |language=zh-tw |chapter=宮之下 - 台灣神社下的火車站|year=2000 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |title=第四章 日治時期臺灣各級神社的選址與設域的空間特性 |pages=137–140 |url=http://rportal.lib.ntnu.edu.tw/bitstream/20.500.12235/94400/4/n088723001004.pdf |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |publisher=[[National Taiwan Normal University]] |language=zh-tw}}</ref> The construction lasted between 1900 and 1901, and the completed shrine was dedicated to Yoshihisa and the {{nihongo|Kaitaku Sanjin|開拓三神|Three Kami Deities of Pioneering}}.<ref name="hsu">{{cite web |author1=許書維 |title=臺灣神社誌 |url=https://www.th.gov.tw/epaper/site/page/78/1078 |website=國史館臺灣文獻館 |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw}}</ref> In 1915, a railway station named {{nihongo|Miyanoshita Station|宮ノ下乘降場||now {{ill|Jiantan metro station|en|Jiantan metro station|WD=Q698433|lt=Jiantan metro station}}}} was placed at the foot of Jiantan Mountain to serve the shrine.<ref name="lee"/> On April 12, 1923, [[:en:Crown Prince|Crown Prince]] {{ill|Hirohito|en|Hirohito|WD=Q34479|lt=Hirohito}}, who would become Emperor Shōwa three years later, embarked on a two-week tour of Taiwan. In preparation for his visit to the shrine, {{nihongo|Chokushi Road|勅使街道|Chokushi kaidō|present-day Chungshan North Road}} was created leading up to the shrine from the city, with the {{nihongo|Meiji Bridge|明治橋}} crossing the Keelung River.<ref>{{cite web |author1=一流人 |title=我們住在「中國的地圖」裡？台北路名藏著滿滿的台灣過往 |url=https://www.gvm.com.tw/article/71003 |website=遠見 |publisher=天下文化 |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw |date=February 21, 2020}}</ref>
The shrine was elevated in Rank to Grand Shrine (''jingū'') in 1944 ({{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa}} 19) when [[Amaterasu]] was enshrined, making it the highest-Ranking shrine in Taiwan.<ref name="hsu"/> The opening ceremony of the new shrine was planned to be on October 28. However, on October 23, 1944, a cargo plane lost control and crashed atop the mountain where the Taiwan Grand Shrine was located, heavily damaging roughly half of the shrine.<ref>{{cite web |author1=郭俊麟 |title=淺談臺灣今昔地圖與歷史航照在中學地理資訊教育的意義 |url=http://epaper.ntu.edu.tw/view.php?id=10773 |website=GIS 教育推廣 電子報 |publisher=[[National Taiwan University]] |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw}}</ref> The shrine was never fully repaired due to {{ill|Surrender of Japan|en|Surrender of Japan|WD=Q6540361|lt=Japan's surrender after World War II}}, and much of the shrine's materials were taken for construction projects elsewhere. In its place, the [[:en:Grand Hotel (Taipei City)|Grand Hotel]] was constructed in 1952, where it remains as a prominent landmark of the city.<ref name="hsu"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Taiwan Grand Shrine.jpg|A {{ill|Postcard|en|Postcard|WD=Q192425|lt=Postcard}} issued by the {{ill|Governor-General of Taiwan|en|Governor-General of Taiwan|WD=Q701172|lt=Governor-General of Taiwan}} of Taiwan Grand Shrine. The [[:en:Imperial Seal of Japan|Imperial Seal]] is visible in the upper-right section. File:Taiwan_(Japanese_Colony)_1932_bank_note_-_10_yen_(front).jpg|Taiwan banknotes (10 yen banknotes) during the Japanese colonial era with the Taiwan Grand Shrine printed File:1923rd year 日本皇太子裕仁訪拜臺灣神社_Japanese_Crown_Prince_Hirohito_on_the_way_to_Taiwan_Grand_Shrine.jpg|Prince Hirohito visits the Taiwan Grand Shrine during the Crown Prince Hirohito's visit to Taiwan (April 17, 1923) File:Copper cattle of Taiwan Jinja.JPG|This copper bull was originally located at Taiwan Grand Shrine but was moved to in front of [[National Taiwan Museum]] after the end of World War II. </gallery>
==See also== *[[:en:Shintoism in Taiwan|Shintoism in Taiwan]] *{{ill|List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan|en|List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan|WD=Q6596888|lt=List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan}}

{{short description|Former Shinto shrine in Taipei, Taiwan}} [[File:Painting of Taiwan Grand Shrine.jpg|thumb|300px|A painting of Taiwan Grand Shrine during {{ill|Taiwan under Japanese rule|en|Taiwan under Japanese rule|WD=Q137816|lt=Japanese colonial rule}}. At the bottom of the painting is the Keelung River and in the lower-right corner the Meiji Bridge is partially visible.]]
The '''Taiwan Grand Shrine''' ({{CJKV|c=臺灣神宮|r=Taiwan Jingū|p=Táiwān Shéngōng}}) was the highest Ranking Japanese {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} in Taiwan during {{ill|Taiwan under Japanese rule|en|Taiwan under Japanese rule|WD=Q137816|lt=Japanese colonial rule}}. It was located in {{ill|Taipei|en|Taipei|WD=Q1867|lt=Taihoku}}, {{ill|Taiwan|en|Taiwan|WD=Q865|lt=Taiwan}} (now {{ill|Zhongshan District, Taipei|en|Zhongshan District, Taipei|WD=Q271167|lt=Zhongshan District, Taipei}}). Among the officially sanctioned Shinto shrines in Taiwan, Taiwan Grand Shrine held the highest Rank of them all. The [[:en:Grand Hotel (Taipei City)|Grand Hotel]] stands at the shrine's former site.
== History == [[File:Taiwan-Grand-Shrine-Jingu-Map-1920s.png|thumb|300px|Taiwan Grand Shrine Map (1920s) records the location of each object and detailed information.]] Following the death of {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|en|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|WD=Q3197569|lt=Prince Yoshihisa}} in 1895, the {{ill|Governor-General of Taiwan|en|Governor-General of Taiwan|WD=Q701172|lt=Governor-General of Taiwan}} [[Nogi Maresuke]] began planning for a shrine in Yoshihisa's honor. Originally, the plan was to construct the shrine at Yuanshan Park ({{lang|zh-Hant|圓山公園}}, now part of {{ill|Taipei Expo Park|en|Taipei Expo Park|WD=Q14197588|lt=Taipei Expo Park}}); however, Nogi's successor {{ill|Kodama Gentarō|en|Kodama Gentarō|WD=Q72515|lt=Kodama Gentarō}} and chief planner {{ill|Gotō Shinpei|en|Gotō Shinpei|WD=Q562492|lt=Gotō Shinpei}} decided to move it across the {{ill|Keelung River|en|Keelung River|WD=Q708976|lt=Keelung River}} to Jiantan Mountain ({{lang|zh-Hant|劍潭山}}) for the site's higher elevation. The vantage point would allow the shrine to overlook the entire city, making it symbolic for the Japanese Empire's colonial power.<ref name="lee">{{cite book |author=李東明 |title=永遠的北淡線 |publisher=玉山社 |isbn=957-8246-32-3 |pages=88–90 |language=zh-tw |chapter=宮之下 - 台灣神社下的火車站|year=2000 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |title=第四章 日治時期臺灣各級神社的選址與設域的空間特性 |pages=137–140 |url=http://rportal.lib.ntnu.edu.tw/bitstream/20.500.12235/94400/4/n088723001004.pdf |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |publisher=[[National Taiwan Normal University]] |language=zh-tw}}</ref> The construction lasted between 1900 and 1901, and the completed shrine was dedicated to Yoshihisa and the {{nihongo|Kaitaku Sanjin|開拓三神|Three Kami Deities of Pioneering}}.<ref name="hsu">{{cite web |author1=許書維 |title=臺灣神社誌 |url=https://www.th.gov.tw/epaper/site/page/78/1078 |website=國史館臺灣文獻館 |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw}}</ref> In 1915, a railway station named {{nihongo|Miyanoshita Station|宮ノ下乘降場||now {{ill|Jiantan metro station|en|Jiantan metro station|WD=Q698433|lt=Jiantan metro station}}}} was placed at the foot of Jiantan Mountain to serve the shrine.<ref name="lee"/> On April 12, 1923, [[:en:Crown Prince|Crown Prince]] {{ill|Hirohito|en|Hirohito|WD=Q34479|lt=Hirohito}}, who would become Emperor Shōwa three years later, embarked on a two-week tour of Taiwan. In preparation for his visit to the shrine, {{nihongo|Chokushi Road|勅使街道|Chokushi kaidō|present-day Chungshan North Road}} was created leading up to the shrine from the city, with the {{nihongo|Meiji Bridge|明治橋}} crossing the Keelung River.<ref>{{cite web |author1=一流人 |title=我們住在「中國的地圖」裡？台北路名藏著滿滿的台灣過往 |url=https://www.gvm.com.tw/article/71003 |website=遠見 |publisher=天下文化 |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw |date=February 21, 2020}}</ref>
The shrine was elevated in Rank to Grand Shrine (''jingū'') in 1944 ({{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa}} 19) when [[Amaterasu]] was enshrined, making it the highest-Ranking shrine in Taiwan.<ref name="hsu"/> The opening ceremony of the new shrine was planned to be on October 28. However, on October 23, 1944, a cargo plane lost control and crashed atop the mountain where the Taiwan Grand Shrine was located, heavily damaging roughly half of the shrine.<ref>{{cite web |author1=郭俊麟 |title=淺談臺灣今昔地圖與歷史航照在中學地理資訊教育的意義 |url=http://epaper.ntu.edu.tw/view.php?id=10773 |website=GIS 教育推廣 電子報 |publisher=[[National Taiwan University]] |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw}}</ref> The shrine was never fully repaired due to {{ill|Surrender of Japan|en|Surrender of Japan|WD=Q6540361|lt=Japan's surrender after World War II}}, and much of the shrine's materials were taken for construction projects elsewhere. In its place, the [[:en:Grand Hotel (Taipei City)|Grand Hotel]] was constructed in 1952, where it remains as a prominent landmark of the city.<ref name="hsu"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Taiwan Grand Shrine.jpg|A {{ill|Postcard|en|Postcard|WD=Q192425|lt=Postcard}} issued by the {{ill|Governor-General of Taiwan|en|Governor-General of Taiwan|WD=Q701172|lt=Governor-General of Taiwan}} of Taiwan Grand Shrine. The [[:en:Imperial Seal of Japan|Imperial Seal]] is visible in the upper-right section. File:Taiwan_(Japanese_Colony)_1932_bank_note_-_10_yen_(front).jpg|Taiwan banknotes (10 yen banknotes) during the Japanese colonial era with the Taiwan Grand Shrine printed File:1923rd year 日本皇太子裕仁訪拜臺灣神社_Japanese_Crown_Prince_Hirohito_on_the_way_to_Taiwan_Grand_Shrine.jpg|Prince Hirohito visits the Taiwan Grand Shrine during the Crown Prince Hirohito's visit to Taiwan (April 17, 1923) File:Copper cattle of Taiwan Jinja.JPG|This copper bull was originally located at Taiwan Grand Shrine but was moved to in front of [[National Taiwan Museum]] after the end of World War II. </gallery>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:ضريح تايوان الكبير]][[co:commons:Category:Taiwan Grand Shrine]][[en:Taiwan Grand Shrine]][[es:Gran Santuario de Taiwán]][[fa:معبد بزرگ تایوان]][[fr:Taiwan-jingū]][[it:Grande Santuario di Taiwan]][[ja:台湾神宮]][[ko:타이완 신궁]][[zh-min-nan:Tâi-oân Sîn-kiong]][[zh:臺灣神宮]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:ضريح تايوان الكبير]][[co:commons:Category:Taiwan Grand Shrine]][[en:Taiwan Grand Shrine]][[es:Gran Santuario de Taiwán]][[fa:معبد بزرگ تایوان]][[fr:Taiwan-jingū]][[it:Grande Santuario di Taiwan]][[ja:台湾神宮]][[ko:타이완 신궁]][[zh-min-nan:Tâi-oân Sîn-kiong]][[zh:臺灣神宮]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:ضريح تايوان الكبير]][[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Taiwan Grand Shrine]][[en:Taiwan Grand Shrine]][[es:Gran Santuario de Taiwán]][[fa:معبد بزرگ تایوان]][[fr:Taiwan-jingū]][[it:Grande Santuario di Taiwan]][[ja:台湾神宮]][[ko:타이완 신궁]][[zh-min-nan:Tâi-oân Sîn-kiong]][[zh:臺灣神宮]]
{{short description|Former Shinto shrine in Taipei, Taiwan}} [[File:Painting of Taiwan Grand Shrine.jpg|thumb|300px|A painting of Taiwan Grand Shrine during {{ill|Taiwan under Japanese rule|en|Taiwan under Japanese rule|WD=Q137816|lt=Japanese colonial rule|1=Taiwan under Japanese rule}}. At the bottom of the painting is the Keelung River and in the lower-right corner the Meiji Bridge is partially visible.]]
The '''Taiwan Grand Shrine''' ({{CJKV|c=臺灣神宮|r=Taiwan Jingū|p=Táiwān Shéngōng}}) was the highest Ranking Japanese {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} in Taiwan during {{ill|Taiwan under Japanese rule|en|Taiwan under Japanese rule|WD=Q137816|lt=Japanese colonial rule|1=Taiwan under Japanese rule}}. It was located in {{ill|Taipei|en|Taipei|WD=Q1867|lt=Taihoku|1=Taipei}}, {{ill|Taiwan|en|Taiwan|WD=Q865|lt=Taiwan|1=Taiwan}} (now {{ill|Zhongshan District, Taipei|en|Zhongshan District, Taipei|WD=Q271167|lt=Zhongshan District, Taipei|1=Zhongshan District, Taipei}}). Among the officially sanctioned Shinto shrines in Taiwan, Taiwan Grand Shrine held the highest Rank of them all. The [[:en:Grand Hotel (Taipei City)|Grand Hotel]] stands at the shrine's former site.
== History == [[File:Taiwan-Grand-Shrine-Jingu-Map-1920s.png|thumb|300px|Taiwan Grand Shrine Map (1920s) records the location of each object and detailed information.]] Following the death of {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|en|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|WD=Q3197569|lt=Prince Yoshihisa|1=Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa}} in 1895, the {{ill|Governor-General of Taiwan|en|Governor-General of Taiwan|WD=Q701172|lt=Governor-General of Taiwan|1=Governor-General of Taiwan}} [[Nogi Maresuke]] began planning for a shrine in Yoshihisa's honor. Originally, the plan was to construct the shrine at Yuanshan Park ({{lang|zh-Hant|圓山公園}}, now part of {{ill|Taipei Expo Park|en|Taipei Expo Park|WD=Q14197588|lt=Taipei Expo Park|1=Taipei Expo Park}}); however, Nogi's successor {{ill|Kodama Gentarō|en|Kodama Gentarō|WD=Q72515|lt=Kodama Gentarō|1=Kodama Gentarō}} and chief planner {{ill|Gotō Shinpei|en|Gotō Shinpei|WD=Q562492|lt=Gotō Shinpei|1=Gotō Shinpei}} decided to move it across the {{ill|Keelung River|en|Keelung River|WD=Q708976|lt=Keelung River|1=Keelung River}} to Jiantan Mountain ({{lang|zh-Hant|劍潭山}}) for the site's higher elevation. The vantage point would allow the shrine to overlook the entire city, making it symbolic for the Japanese Empire's colonial power.<ref name="lee">{{cite book |author=李東明 |title=永遠的北淡線 |publisher=玉山社 |isbn=957-8246-32-3 |pages=88–90 |language=zh-tw |chapter=宮之下 - 台灣神社下的火車站|year=2000 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |title=第四章 日治時期臺灣各級神社的選址與設域的空間特性 |pages=137–140 |url=http://rportal.lib.ntnu.edu.tw/bitstream/20.500.12235/94400/4/n088723001004.pdf |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |publisher=[[National Taiwan Normal University]] |language=zh-tw}}</ref> The construction lasted between 1900 and 1901, and the completed shrine was dedicated to Yoshihisa and the {{nihongo|Kaitaku Sanjin|開拓三神|Three Kami Deities of Pioneering}}.<ref name="hsu">{{cite web |author1=許書維 |title=臺灣神社誌 |url=https://www.th.gov.tw/epaper/site/page/78/1078 |website=國史館臺灣文獻館 |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw}}</ref> In 1915, a railway station named {{nihongo|Miyanoshita Station|宮ノ下乘降場||now {{ill|Jiantan metro station|en|Jiantan metro station|WD=Q698433|lt=Jiantan metro station|1=Jiantan metro station}}}} was placed at the foot of Jiantan Mountain to serve the shrine.<ref name="lee"/> On April 12, 1923, [[:en:Crown Prince|Crown Prince]] {{ill|Hirohito|en|Hirohito|WD=Q34479|lt=Hirohito|1=Hirohito}}, who would become Emperor Shōwa three years later, embarked on a two-week tour of Taiwan. In preparation for his visit to the shrine, {{nihongo|Chokushi Road|勅使街道|Chokushi kaidō|present-day Chungshan North Road}} was created leading up to the shrine from the city, with the {{nihongo|Meiji Bridge|明治橋}} crossing the Keelung River.<ref>{{cite web |author1=一流人 |title=我們住在「中國的地圖」裡？台北路名藏著滿滿的台灣過往 |url=https://www.gvm.com.tw/article/71003 |website=遠見 |publisher=天下文化 |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw |date=February 21, 2020}}</ref>
The shrine was elevated in Rank to Grand Shrine (''jingū'') in 1944 ({{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa era}} 19) when [[Amaterasu]] was enshrined, making it the highest-Ranking shrine in Taiwan.<ref name="hsu"/> The opening ceremony of the new shrine was planned to be on October 28. However, on October 23, 1944, a cargo plane lost control and crashed atop the mountain where the Taiwan Grand Shrine was located, heavily damaging roughly half of the shrine.<ref>{{cite web |author1=郭俊麟 |title=淺談臺灣今昔地圖與歷史航照在中學地理資訊教育的意義 |url=http://epaper.ntu.edu.tw/view.php?id=10773 |website=GIS 教育推廣 電子報 |publisher=[[National Taiwan University]] |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw}}</ref> The shrine was never fully repaired due to {{ill|Surrender of Japan|en|Surrender of Japan|WD=Q6540361|lt=Japan's surrender after World War II|1=Surrender of Japan}}, and much of the shrine's materials were taken for construction projects elsewhere. In its place, the [[:en:Grand Hotel (Taipei City)|Grand Hotel]] was constructed in 1952, where it remains as a prominent landmark of the city.<ref name="hsu"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Taiwan Grand Shrine.jpg|A {{ill|Postcard|en|Postcard|WD=Q192425|lt=Postcard|1=Postcard}} issued by the {{ill|Governor-General of Taiwan|en|Governor-General of Taiwan|WD=Q701172|lt=Governor-General of Taiwan|1=Governor-General of Taiwan}} of Taiwan Grand Shrine. The [[:en:Imperial Seal of Japan|Imperial Seal]] is visible in the upper-right section. File:Taiwan_(Japanese_Colony)_1932_bank_note_-_10_yen_(front).jpg|Taiwan banknotes (10 yen banknotes) during the Japanese colonial era with the Taiwan Grand Shrine printed File:1923rd year 日本皇太子裕仁訪拜臺灣神社_Japanese_Crown_Prince_Hirohito_on_the_way_to_Taiwan_Grand_Shrine.jpg|Prince Hirohito visits the Taiwan Grand Shrine during the Crown Prince Hirohito's visit to Taiwan (April 17, 1923) File:Copper cattle of Taiwan Jinja.JPG|This copper bull was originally located at Taiwan Grand Shrine but was moved to in front of [[National Taiwan Museum]] after the end of World War II. </gallery>
==See also== *[[:en:Shintoism in Taiwan|Shintoism in Taiwan]] *{{ill|List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan|en|List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan|WD=Q6596888|lt=List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan|1=List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan}}

Among the officially sanctioned Shinto shrines in Taiwan, Taiwan Grand Shrine held the highest Rank of them all. The [[:en:Grand Hotel (Taipei City)|Grand Hotel]] stands at the shrine's former site.
== History == [[File:Taiwan-Grand-Shrine-Jingu-Map-1920s.png|thumb|300px|Taiwan Grand Shrine Map (1920s) records the location of each object and detailed information.]] Following the death of {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|en|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|WD=Q3197569|lt=Prince Yoshihisa|1=Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa}} in 1895, the {{ill|Governor-General of Taiwan|en|Governor-General of Taiwan|WD=Q701172|lt=Governor-General of Taiwan|1=Governor-General of Taiwan}} [[Nogi Maresuke]] began planning for a shrine in Yoshihisa's honor. Originally, the plan was to construct the shrine at Yuanshan Park ({{lang|zh-Hant|圓山公園}}, now part of {{ill|Taipei Expo Park|en|Taipei Expo Park|WD=Q14197588|lt=Taipei Expo Park|1=Taipei Expo Park}}); however, Nogi's successor {{ill|Kodama Gentarō|en|Kodama Gentarō|WD=Q72515|lt=Kodama Gentarō|1=Kodama Gentarō}} and chief planner {{ill|Gotō Shinpei|en|Gotō Shinpei|WD=Q562492|lt=Gotō Shinpei|1=Gotō Shinpei}} decided to move it across the {{ill|Keelung River|en|Keelung River|WD=Q708976|lt=Keelung River|1=Keelung River}} to Jiantan Mountain ({{lang|zh-Hant|劍潭山}}) for the site's higher elevation. The vantage point would allow the shrine to overlook the entire city, making it symbolic for the Japanese Empire's colonial power.<ref name="lee">{{cite book |author=李東明 |title=永遠的北淡線 |publisher=玉山社 |isbn=957-8246-32-3 |pages=88–90 |language=zh-tw |chapter=宮之下 - 台灣神社下的火車站|year=2000 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |title=第四章 日治時期臺灣各級神社的選址與設域的空間特性 |pages=137–140 |url=http://rportal.lib.ntnu.edu.tw/bitstream/20.500.12235/94400/4/n088723001004.pdf |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |publisher=[[National Taiwan Normal University]] |language=zh-tw}}</ref> The construction lasted between 1900 and 1901, and the completed shrine was dedicated to Yoshihisa and the {{nihongo|Kaitaku Sanjin|開拓三神|Three Kami Deities of Pioneering}}.<ref name="hsu">{{cite web |author1=許書維 |title=臺灣神社誌 |url=https://www.th.gov.tw/epaper/site/page/78/1078 |website=國史館臺灣文獻館 |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw}}</ref> In 1915, a railway station named {{nihongo|Miyanoshita Station|宮ノ下乘降場||now {{ill|Jiantan metro station|en|Jiantan metro station|WD=Q698433|lt=Jiantan metro station|1=Jiantan metro station}}}} was placed at the foot of Jiantan Mountain to serve the shrine.<ref name="lee"/> On April 12, 1923, [[:en:Crown Prince|Crown Prince]] {{ill|Hirohito|en|Hirohito|WD=Q34479|lt=Hirohito|1=Hirohito}}, who would become Emperor Shōwa three years later, embarked on a two-week tour of Taiwan. In preparation for his visit to the shrine, {{nihongo|Chokushi Road|勅使街道|Chokushi kaidō|present-day Chungshan North Road}} was created leading up to the shrine from the city, with the {{nihongo|Meiji Bridge|明治橋}} crossing the Keelung River.<ref>{{cite web |author1=一流人 |title=我們住在「中國的地圖」裡？台北路名藏著滿滿的台灣過往 |url=https://www.gvm.com.tw/article/71003 |website=遠見 |publisher=天下文化 |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw |date=February 21, 2020}}</ref>
The shrine was elevated in Rank to Grand Shrine (''jingū'') in 1944 ({{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa era}} 19) when [[Amaterasu]] was enshrined, making it the highest-Ranking shrine in Taiwan.<ref name="hsu"/> The opening ceremony of the new shrine was planned to be on October 28. However, on October 23, 1944, a cargo plane lost control and crashed atop the mountain where the Taiwan Grand Shrine was located, heavily damaging roughly half of the shrine.<ref>{{cite web |author1=郭俊麟 |title=淺談臺灣今昔地圖與歷史航照在中學地理資訊教育的意義 |url=http://epaper.ntu.edu.tw/view.php?id=10773 |website=GIS 教育推廣 電子報 |publisher=[[National Taiwan University]] |accessdate=May 31, 2020 |language=zh-tw}}</ref> The shrine was never fully repaired due to {{ill|Surrender of Japan|en|Surrender of Japan|WD=Q6540361|lt=Japan's surrender after World War II|1=Surrender of Japan}}, and much of the shrine's materials were taken for construction projects elsewhere. In its place, the [[:en:Grand Hotel (Taipei City)|Grand Hotel]] was constructed in 1952, where it remains as a prominent landmark of the city.<ref name="hsu"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Taiwan Grand Shrine.jpg|A {{ill|Postcard|en|Postcard|WD=Q192425|lt=Postcard|1=Postcard}} issued by the {{ill|Governor-General of Taiwan|en|Governor-General of Taiwan|WD=Q701172|lt=Governor-General of Taiwan|1=Governor-General of Taiwan}} of Taiwan Grand Shrine. The [[:en:Imperial Seal of Japan|Imperial Seal]] is visible in the upper-right section. File:Taiwan_(Japanese_Colony)_1932_bank_note_-_10_yen_(front).jpg|Taiwan banknotes (10 yen banknotes) during the Japanese colonial era with the Taiwan Grand Shrine printed File:1923rd year 日本皇太子裕仁訪拜臺灣神社_Japanese_Crown_Prince_Hirohito_on_the_way_to_Taiwan_Grand_Shrine.jpg|Prince Hirohito visits the Taiwan Grand Shrine during the Crown Prince Hirohito's visit to Taiwan (April 17, 1923) File:Copper cattle of Taiwan Jinja.JPG|This copper bull was originally located at Taiwan Grand Shrine but was moved to in front of [[National Taiwan Museum]] after the end of World War II. </gallery>
==See also== *[[:en:Shintoism in Taiwan|Shintoism in Taiwan]] *{{ill|List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan|en|List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan|WD=Q6596888|lt=List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan|1=List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan}}

At the bottom of the painting is the Keelung River and in the lower-right corner the Meiji Bridge is partially visible.]]

In its place, the [[:en:Grand Hotel (Taipei City)|Grand Hotel]] was constructed in 1952, where it remains as a prominent landmark of the city.<ref name="hsu"/>

The [[:en:Imperial Seal of Japan|Imperial Seal]] is visible in the upper-right section. File:Taiwan_(Japanese_Colony)_1932_bank_note_-_10_yen_(front).jpg|Taiwan banknotes (10 yen banknotes) during the Japanese colonial era with the Taiwan Grand Shrine printed File:1923rd year 日本皇太子裕仁訪拜臺灣神社_Japanese_Crown_Prince_Hirohito_on_the_way_to_Taiwan_Grand_Shrine.jpg|Prince Hirohito visits the Taiwan Grand Shrine during the Crown Prince Hirohito's visit to Taiwan (April 17, 1923) File:Copper cattle of Taiwan Jinja.JPG|This copper bull was originally located at Taiwan Grand Shrine but was moved to in front of [[National Taiwan Museum]] after the end of World War II. </gallery>
